issue_title: Version scheme bug in pre-release 0.7.1-beta1
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

This issue is both concerned with a missing procedure instruction, execution, and an actual bug :)

1. Missing procedure execution:

After the release on 0.7.0, the branch was left at:

    VERSION = (0, 7, 0, 'final', 0)

This will mean that all releases think that they are final and skip the pre-release behavior for version detection -- both from VERSION files and git data.

2. Missing instruction 

After a release, the branch needs to be bumped to the next development stage. This should have been mentioned in the [Post-release TODO](http://kolibri-dev.readthedocs.io/en/develop/references/release_process.html#post-release-todo)

    VERSION = (0, 7, 1, 'alpha', 0)

3: Bug

Additionally, if you release with a `kolibri/VERSION` file with `0.7.1b1`, and it was based on the above `VERSION` tuple, we hit another problem:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".virtualenvs/kolibri/bin/kolibri", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('kolibri', 'console_scripts', 'kolibri')()
  File ".virtualenvs/kolibri/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 542, in load_entry_point
    return get_distribution(dist).load_entry_point(group, name)
  File ".virtualenvs/kolibri/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2569, in load_entry_point
    return ep.load()
  File ".virtualenvs/kolibri/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2229, in load
    return self.resolve()
  File ".virtualenvs/kolibri/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2235, in resolve
    module = __import__(self.module_name, fromlist=['__name__'], level=0)
  File "/kolibri/__init__.py", line 10, in <module>
    __version__ = str(get_version(VERSION))
  File "/kolibri/utils/lru_cache.py", line 137, in wrapper
    result = user_function(*args, **kwds)
  File "/kolibri/utils/version.py", line 372, in get_version
    return get_prerelease_version(version)
  File "/kolibri/utils/version.py", line 343, in get_prerelease_version
    version_file
AssertionError: VERSION file inconsistent with kolibri.__version__. __version__ is: [0, 7, 1, u'alpha', 0], File says: 0.7.1b1
```

### Expected behavior

In our latest release, the version should be `0.7.1b1` because of the tag, but it reports as `0.7.0`.

Finally, this issue is also related to #2762 

### User-facing consequences

Makes life confusion for pre-release testers... so would suggest that we fix it for the beta2 just to ensure that stuff is working :)

### Errors and logs

### Context

issue_title: Username validation accepts hyphens ('-') when it shouldn't
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Josh in the Discourse forum ran into problems because his superuser username had a dash.

https://community.learningequality.org/t/kolibri-setup-issue/426/3

Confirmed that the setup wizard (and probably everything else using the username validation) accepts usernames with hyphens, which leads to a 400 error when creating a new user.

@radinamatic 

### Expected behavior

The setup wizard does not allow usernames with hyphens in them. Also, it would be nicer if this error was handled inside of the Setup Wizard step instead of showing a generic "something went wrong" error screen to provide some hints as to what happened.

### User-facing consequences

More users might run into this, especially since the invalid username text only appears when input is "invalid".


### Steps to reproduce

1. In the setup wizard try to create a superuser with a '-' in the username
1. Submit it and see the error screen

### Context
 * Kolibri version: 0.7
 * Operating system: any
 * Browser: any

issue_title: Don't set dirty bit when only updating MPTT fields (which aren't serialized)
issue_body:
By default, when we do a save/update on a syncable model, we set the dirty bit. When a node in an MPTT tree is moved, django-mptt often [updates the MPTT fields (left/right/level/tree_id)](https://github.com/django-mptt/django-mptt/blob/8bcbe20984fcdcb85dabb6588bf0c423ff1209bf/mptt/managers.py#L690) for a number of other nodes as well. Since we'll be excluding these MPTT fields from serialization/syncing, there's no need to set the dirty bit.

The easiest way to do this looks like it would be overriding [`_mptt_update`](https://github.com/django-mptt/django-mptt/blob/8bcbe20984fcdcb85dabb6588bf0c423ff1209bf/mptt/managers.py#L413) and adding the flag to not set the dirty bit (the `None`) option, on our `SyncableMPTTTreeManager` or whatever we're calling it.

issue_title: Sqlalchemy doesn't update nested elements in objects
issue_body:
Using sqlalchemy is not possible to update a nested element. 

#  Example

Model:

```
>>> from crate.client.sqlalchemy.types import Object, ObjectArray
>>> from uuid import uuid4

>>> class Character(Base):
...     __tablename__ = 'characters'
...     id = sa.Column(sa.String, primary_key=True, default=gen_key)
...     name = sa.Column(sa.String)
...     quote = sa.Column(sa.String)
...     details = sa.Column(Object)
...     more_details = sa.Column(ObjectArray)
...     name_ft = sa.Column(sa.String)
...     quote_ft = sa.Column(sa.String)
```

We try to update a Character:

```
>>> char = session.query(Character).filter_by(name='Arthur Dent').one()
>>> char.details['gender'] = 'manly man'
>>> char.details['field_A']['sub_field_a'] = 'value_aaaa'
>>> session.commit()
>>> session.refresh(char)
```

SQLAlchemy generates the following query:

```
UPDATE characters SET details['gender'] = 'manly man' WHERE analysis.id = '955ce9c7-e85f-489d-b48c-bf6e216c10fe';
```
But char.details['field_A']['sub_field_a'] is not taken in account in the query. The expected query is:

```
UPDATE characters SET details['gender'] = 'manly man', details['field_A']['sub_field_a'] = 'value_aaaa' WHERE analysis.id = '955ce9c7-e85f-489d-b48c-bf6e216c10fe';
```


#  Versions:
Python 2.7
Crate Data Python client 0.16.3
SQLalchemy 0.9.9

issue_title: Minor refactoring due to what it seems a fail in the Digi Xbee documentation
issue_body:
** What steps will reproduce the problem? **

1. Trying to issue an 'ND' command (API mode) and later parsing it (the problem is in the parsing).
For example: 
    xbee.send("at", frame='A', command='ND')
    print xbee.wait_read_frame()

** What is the expected output? What do you see instead? **

You were expected to receive the following information associated with the ND command refered in the datasheet:

QUOTE:

Node Discover. Discovers and reports all RF modules found. The following information is reported for each module discovered.
MY<CR>
SH<CR>
SL<CR>
NI<CR> (Variable length)
PARENT_NETWORK ADDRESS (2 Bytes)<CR>
DEVICE_TYPE<CR> (1 Byte: 0=Coord, 1=Router, 2=End Device)
STATUS<CR> (1 Byte: Reserved)
PROFILE_ID<CR> (2 Bytes)
CRE
MANUFACTURER_ID<CR> (2 Bytes)
<CR>
If ND is sent through the API, each response is returned as a separate
AT_CMD_Response packet. The data consists of the above listed bytes without the carriage return delimiters. The NI string will end in a "0x00" null character. The radius of the ND command is set by the BH command.

END QUOTE:

Instead it seems like the module sends you additional data (4 bytes) that are not refered in the datasheet. To me these 4 bytes seem to be additional info refering to the command 'DD' (Device identifier type). This makes the method "_parse_ND_at_response(self, packet_info)" send an Error message.


** What version of the product are you using? On what operating system? **
I m using the Xbee Zigbee Pro S2B, later i will test this with Xbee Zigbee S2B

Please provide any additional information below.

You can solve this adding the following line (in method _parse_ND_at_response(self, packet_info)):

"result['device_type_identifier'] = packet_info['parameter'][null_terminator_index+9:null_terminator_index+13]"

after the line:

"result['manufacturer'] = packet_info['parameter'][null_terminator_index+7:null_terminator_index+9]"

and modifying the test before return of the method to:

"               if null_terminator_index+13 != len(packet_info['parameter']):
                   raise ValueError("Improper ND response length: expected {0}, read {1} bytes".format(len(packet_info['parameter']), null_terminator_index+9))
"

The correct way to solve probably would be to take this last test out, and believe in the checksum. This way if Digi decides to add more information to the DATA packet you won t have problems and will only parse it if necessary

issue_title: Tweaks to ipwb ServiceWorker support, fallbacks, and compatibility
issue_body:
The ipwb replay system uses service workers for URI-M rewriting. Some browsers don't yet support service worker. At the very least, we should provide a message in the UI when SW registration fails.

A further approach would be to have URI-writing as a fallback, which is really messy and problematic.

As a further use case, service workers currently require either HTTPS or localhost. We have been testing on localhost but if someone wanted to deploy this a different host sans HTTPS, SW would not work.

We could say that we only support browsers that support SW and only SWs on HTTPS -- excluding these other scenarios would make testing easier -- but also means ipwb breaks for some users.

We can snub it or mitigate it.

issue_title: Create a new endpoint to return a matched CDXJ record
issue_body:
We need a new endpoint to return an index record instead of a reconstructed memento. This will enable us to try fetching IPFS blocks directly from the SW and reconstruct the memento there instead of letting the server do this. This will be a step in the direction of server-free decentralized replay. This will eliminate the need of threading as we can leverage asynchronous nature of JS for concurrent fetches. Additionally, we can avoid the location header rewriting issue (as per #456 and #461) by reusing the logic already present in Reconstructive.

`/cdxj/:datetime/:urir` should return 404 if no record is found, but return 200 (not a 3xx) otherwise with the one entry extracted from the index. We can either return `application/cdxj+ors` content type or `application/json` if we transform the index record into JSON.

issue_title: Python API - bug caused by compatibility issues with the tables module
issue_body:
I got this error using the method createMapping() from the Python API:

```
--> 172         mymap = self.createArray(self.root.lookup, title, atom=tables.UInt32Atom(),AttributeError: 'File' object has no attribute 'createArray'`
It turns out that in my "tables" module (version 3.3.0) the method createArray() is called crate_array().
```

I fixed this bug localy by manually changing one line in the `createMapping(self, title, entries, overwrite=False)` method of the `File` class.

The original code was:
```
# Write the mapping!
mymap = self.createArray(self.root.lookup, title, atom=tables.UInt32Atom(),
shape=(len(entries),) )
```
I replaced it by:

```
# Write the mapping!
mymap = self.create_array(self.root.lookup, title, atom=tables.UInt32Atom(),
shape=(len(entries),) )
```

For information, I am using Python 2.7.3, and the openmatrix version 0.2

issue_title: charm build outputs to trusty if top-level layer doesn't include series
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
charm 2.2.0-0ubuntu1~ubuntu16.04.1~ppa2
charm-tools 2.1.4
```

I am using: Ubuntu Xenial

## Issue/Feature

If a top-level charm layer is depending on a base layer to provide the supported series (e.g., if it's a thin wrapper over the base charm) and thus has no `series` field in its own `metadata.yaml`, when you do `charm build` it doesn't look in the built `metadata.yaml` for a series, so your multi-series charm ends up in `/trusty/`.

When Juju 2.0 is officially released, `/builds/` should be the default, so that the output dir is always consistent.

issue_title: Incompatibility with Python requests < 2.4.3
issue_body:
The method _'_wrapped_request()'_ of the ProfitBricksService class has the attribute _'json'_, which is used in calling the _request()_ method of a requests Session object.

But this argument of the method _request()_ was introduced with version _2.4.3_ of requests, earlier versions of requests did not support this attribute. But e.g. version 2.2.1 of requests is widely used, for instance in Ubuntu 14.04 LTS.

The method _'_wrapped_request()'_ is a (more or less) private method of the ProfitBricksService class, and the parameter _'json'_ of this method is used nowhere inside the ProfitBricksService class. That's why I would propose to remove this argument to stay compatible with Python requests 2.2.1.

issue_title: GELF logging driver support
issue_body:
Docker 1.8 has added native Graylog integration with a GELF logging driver: https://www.graylog.org/blog/28-centralized-docker-container-logging-with-native-graylog-integration.

From the command line, a container can be created with the gelf driver like so: `docker run --log-driver=gelf --log-opt gelf-address=udp://<GraylogIP>:12201 busybox echo Hello Graylog`.

I propose adding support for this driver type into the `create_container` method.

issue_title: Add example in the docs for creating a ComposedSampler
ptyprocess 0.5.1 and 0.5.2 appear to be missing the setup.py file.
Create ClusterGraph class
issue_body:
* Currently we don't have any class in class to represent cluster graph. Create a ```ClusterGraph``` class.
* ```JunctionTree``` class should inherit from ```ClusterGraph``` instead of ```UndirectedGraph``` as junction tree (or clique tree) are a special case of cluster graph.
* Add ```to_cluster_graph()``` method in ```BayesianModel```, ```MarkovModel``` and ```FactorGraph```.

issue_title: `class_names` argument should be required only in combination with certain options
issue_body:
### `class_names` argument should be required only in combination with certain options
Some options do not require `class_names` to be performed, such as `--version` or the newly added `--list-courses`. In such cases, `class_names` should be optional

### Your environment
* Operating System (name/version): OSX
* Python version: 3.5.2
* coursera-dl version: 0.7.0

### Steps to reproduce
```
coursera-dl --version
coursera-dl --list-courses
```

### Expected behaviour
These actions should be performed

### Actual behaviour
The following error message is displayed:
    
```
usage: coursera-dl [-h] [-u USERNAME] [-p PASSWORD] [--jobs JOBS] [-b]
                   [--path PATH] [-sl SUBTITLE_LANGUAGE] [--download-quizzes]
                   [--about] [-f FILE_FORMATS]
                   [--ignore-formats IGNORE_FORMATS] [-sf SECTION_FILTER]
                   [-lf LECTURE_FILTER] [-rf RESOURCE_FILTER]
                   [--video-resolution VIDEO_RESOLUTION]
                   [--disable-url-skipping] [--wget [WGET]] [--curl [CURL]]
                   [--aria2 [ARIA2]] [--axel [AXEL]]
                   [--downloader-arguments DOWNLOADER_ARGUMENTS]
                   [--list-courses] [--resume] [-o] [--verbose-dirs] [--quiet]
                   [-r] [--combined-section-lectures-nums]
                   [--unrestricted-filenames] [-c COOKIES_FILE] [-n [NETRC]]
                   [-k] [--clear-cache] [--hook HOOKS] [-pl] [--skip-download]
                   [--debug] [--cache-syllabus] [--version] [-l LOCAL_PAGE]
                   class_names [class_names ...]
coursera-dl: error: the following arguments are required: class_names
```

Add support to jbossapi to fetch thread pool statistics
issue_body:
If you use a named thread pool in jboss you are able to collect the thread pools statistics using the threads subsystem from the management API.

For example:

```
/management/subsystem/threads?include-runtime=true&recursive=true
```

You end up getting something like this back:

```
{
  "blocking-bounded-queue-thread-pool": null,
  "blocking-queueless-thread-pool": null,
  "queueless-thread-pool": null,
  "scheduled-thread-pool": null,
  "thread-factory": null,
  "unbounded-queue-thread-pool": null,
  "bounded-queue-thread-pool": {

      "allow-core-timeout": false,
      "core-threads": null,
      "current-thread-count": 26,
      "handoff-executor": null,

issue_title: Convention to skip codes in Rmarkdown when running in DSC
issue_body:
Previously we have implemented support to RMD file as DSC executable modules. Thinking about it a bit more, I think we can make some efforts to bridge between interactive analyses and running in DSC if we invent some conventions for RMD indented to be used in DSC. Since I'm not very sure how RMD is typically used I hope you could suggest me what to do. 

Basically, one possibility could be something like this, eg, in DSC:

```yaml
normal: normal.Rmd
  n: 1000
  ...
```

And the Rmd file might look like:

```rmd

Now let's prototype this method. First set parameters:

` ` `{r}
n = 1000
` ` ` 
Then run this method

` ` `{r chunck_name}
x = rnorm(n)
` ` `

where (explain what everything is)

Now let's make a diagnostic plot:

` ` `{r}
plot(x)
` ` `

```


You see DSC will only need the 2nd code chunk.  Does it seem to be useful to have something along the lines of:

```yaml
normal: normal.Rmd:name
  n:1000
```

Or even eg, 

```yaml
trunk_normal: normal.Rmd:simulate + normal.Rmd:truncate
  n: 1000
```
This way will in some way tie DSC with exploratory analysis. If this propose looks useful in practice, could we decide on a syntax for it?

issue_title: Fix alaska scraper to return approximate dates
issue_body:
Alaska has a frustrating website:

http://www.courtrecords.alaska.gov/webdocs/opinions/sp.htm

The problem is that rather than putting the items in the website with actual dates, they do quarterly date spans like:

> July - September 2016

These used to be the exception, I believe, but now they're the norm. So...we need to deal with them.

In https://github.com/freelawproject/courtlistener/issues/533 we're adding a new field to the DB for approximate dates. We should add support for this to Juriscraper as well. I think the process would be:

 - [ ] Update Juriscraper to have a new function, something like, `get_date_truthiness` (<-- That's probably bad). The new function would return True for every date that was known to be accurate, and would return False for ones that are estimates.

 - [ ] Update Alaska to return this value as appropriate. I suppose some of the time they used to provide accurate dates?

 - [x] Update CL to have the needed field in the DB and to show it in appropriate places, maybe with an FAQ and a (?) thing.

 - [x] Update the code that calls Juriscraper in CL so that it sets the values in the DB as we scrape.

issue_title: Non-free file within the project, licensing issue.
issue_body:
Hi,

I tried to upload pulp in Debian, but the package has been rejected by the FTP masters, because of a non-free file. Here's the REJECT message from the FTP masters:

"Also it is rather bad that according to:
 python-pulp-1.6.0+dfsg1/doc/source/CaseStudies/a_set_partitioning_problem.rst
the license of wedding_seating.jpg seems to be CC BY-NC-ND 2.0,"

The CC BY-NC-ND is a non-free license, as it prevents redistribution.

Could you please get rid of this file, or get it re-licensed?

Also, from the Debian FTP masters, they wrote this:
"on a short glimpse I found the following licenses that should be 
mentioned in your debian/copyright:
 Zope
 CC Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 New Zealand License
 Perl Artistic License
 LaTeX Project Public License
 setup.py says the project is under BSD"

This is a lot of licenses. Wouldn't it be possible to have a better (unified) licensing in the project?

issue_title: bug(router): remote etcd hosts not properly configurable
issue_body:
`deis-router` sources its _own_ address _and_ the address of a machine running etcd from the _same_ environment variable:

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/router/boot.go#L39-L49

```
host := getopt("HOST", "127.0.0.1")

// ...

hostEtcdPath := getopt("HOST_ETCD_PATH", "/deis/router/hosts/"+host)

// ...

client := etcd.NewClient([]string{"http://" + host + ":" + etcdPort})
```

This creates the unfortunate limitation of not being able to run the `deis-router` on any machine that isn't also running etcd.

According to CoreOS' own cluster architecture recommendations, it's not advisable to run etcd on more than nine nodes.  This means that large clusters, should run etcd on a small (probably fixed) number of nodes that run "common services," with a more elastic pool of worker nodes reserved for everything else.  In the case of a large Deis cluster, most Deis components probably run along with the "common services" as well.  Notable exceptions include `deis-logspout` and `deis-publisher`, which must run on the worker nodes.  (`deis-logspout` actually needs to run on both.)  I would argue that in a _large_ cluster where the `deis-router` component may need to scale to many instances, an admin might reasonably elect to schedule and scale that component on worker nodes.  But if the worker nodes aren't running etcd (and we've established they would not in such a case) then the code above is problematic.

Reference: https://coreos.com/docs/cluster-management/setup/cluster-architectures/

`deis-logspout` and `deis-publisher` both seem to address this issue by used of a dedicated `ETCD_HOST` environment variable.  The same approach could be used here, although I would argue that all three would benefit from having a comma delimited set of `ETCD_PEERS` specified instead, which can be parsed to an array and passed to `etcd.NewClient(...)` as that would improve their overall resilience.  Today, there would be problems using the host specified by `ETCD_HOST`if that host went offline-- even if the etcd cluster as a whole managed to maintain quorum/health.

issue_title: "deis destroy" doesn't show the 40x error for an unauthorized user
issue_body:
While an unauthorized user cannot actually destroy the app, the deis CLI doesn't report that fact currently. Cosmetic, but annoying:
```console
$ deis destroy -a wilder-zeppelin

 !    WARNING: Potentially Destructive Action
 !    This command will destroy the application: wilder-zeppelin
 !    To proceed, type "wilder-zeppelin" or re-run this command with --confirm=wilder-zeppelin

> wilder-zeppelin
Destroying wilder-zeppelin... 
done in 0s
```

issue_title: aws elb instances ip addresses mismatch
issue_body:
I'm not sure where the issue origin is. I'm not very familiar with cloudformation but from reading provided template and doc it seems to be fine.

But from the beginning I deploy deis on ec2 via provided scripts. Everything is fine, I'm getting list of instances with ELB address:

```
...
Instances are available:
i-b35a5660	<ip1>	m4.large	us-east-1c	running
i-d6ce8e7e	<ip2>	m4.large	us-east-1b	running
i-489203e4	<ip3>	m4.large	us-east-1d	running
Using ELB <elb id> at <elb address>
Your Deis cluster has been successfully deployed to AWS CloudFormation and is started.
Please continue to follow the instructions in the documentation.
Enabling proxy protocol
1
```
Before installation I do quick dig query, strangely I'm getting list o 3 ips, which AREN'T my instances from previous message, I don't even have such instances in my AWS account. Reponse should be empty list, because these instances aren't passing health checks yet (no platform installed!, elb shows 0 operational instances).

```
dig <elb address> +short
<ip4>
<ip5>
<ip6>
```
So I continue install platform, instances are operational now (elb panel shows 3 of 3 up) but dig still shows ips4-6 which AREN'T mine. If I add one more instances dns query shows this one added properly + three instances which I don't know where they come from.

Does anyone have any idea how to handle this? It isn't issue with dns caching, I'm getting same dns responses if query directly amazon's dns servers and additional instances are showing properly.

issue_title: builder will not come up after updating CoreOS on a v1.10.1 cluster
issue_body:
I set up a fresh deis 1.10.1 cluster this morning and deployed two apps with no major problems.
@carmstrong suggested that I upgrade CoreOS on the cluster per #4438.
I followed the [documentation instructions](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/upgrading-deis/?highlight=coreos#upgrading-coreos). `deisctl list` looked good after every instance reboot except the final one. There were no differences in my procedure.

After the final instance reboot, the builder seemed to be death looping. `deisctl journal builder` showed a bunch of the same error over and over. I attempted to restart the builder - no love. I attempted to stop the controller and builder, start the controller, and start the builder - no love. I stopped the builder, controller, registry@1, and store-gateway@1. I then started store-gateway@1, registry@1, and controller without problems, builder still had the same problems. I was able to capture some clean logs for when the errors started.

issue_title: etcd not joining cluster
issue_body:
I have an AWS stateless cluster.

I terminated one instance, and then I used `etcdctl member remove` on the dead node.

Another instance was spawned in its place, eventually. But this new member isn't joining anymore:

```
$ etcdctl cluster-health
member c075c2b2a194c97d is healthy: got healthy result from http://##.##.2.21:2379
member ef7f859394820d8d is healthy: got healthy result from http://##.##.1.66:2379
```

But in this node (.1.227), it seems everything is working fine.

```
ssh core@XX.XX.1.227

$ etcdctl ls
/coreos.com
/deis
/registry
/sshHostKey

$ fleetctl list-machines
MACHINE         IP              METADATA
744acbe0...     XX.XX.1.227     controlPlane=true,dataPlane=true,routerMesh=true

e1a44903...     XX.XX.2.21      controlPlane=true,dataPlane=true,routerMesh=true
```

Any clues on what may be happening?

issue_title: Update fsouza/go-dockerclient vendored package(s)
issue_body: Deis v1's dependencies have rusted somewhat. In particular, logspout would like to take advantage of this fix: fsouza/go-dockerclient#206, but updating that with `godep` is anything but straightforward.

issue_title: adding VMs to GCE cluster
issue_body:
Hi there,
I'm trying to add 2 instances to an already running 3 nodes GCE cluster, just created by following the docs.

To create 2 new disks:
```gcloud compute disks create cored4 cored5 --size 256GB --type pd-standard --zone europe-west1-c```

To create 2 new instances, using the same user-data as the old instances:
```for num in 4 5; do gcloud compute instances create core${num} \ --zone europe-west1-c \ --machine-type n1-standard-2 \ --metadata-from-file user-data=gce-user-data sshKeys=~/.ssh/deis.pub \ --disk name=cored${num} device-name=coredocker \ --tags deis \ --image coreos-stable-607-0-0-v20150317 \ --image-project coreos-cloud; done```

Add the new instances to the target pool
```gcloud compute target-pools add-instances deis --instances core4 core5```

issue_title: Expand adoc description of batch transfer mode + `--recursive` flag
issue_body:
This relates a little to globusonline/docs.globus.org#180

Someone mentioned in a ticket that he found the "Using the CLI" doc inconsistent with the reference.
If my guess is right, this is because we're not sufficiently clear in the reference documentation that command-line `--recursive` and batch-mode `--recursive` are different and mutually exclusive.

We may want to further clarify with a one or two examples on the reference page, as in
```shell
$ globus transfer --batch ...
xyz def
--recursive a b
c d --recursive
```

issue_title: Need a persistent way to keep track of peer details/reputation
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Our `PeerPool` currently provides no API for its clients to choose the _best_  peer when they need to make a request, so they end up picking one randomly.

The `PeerPool` also has to either use a hard-coded list of peers or run the discovery bootstrap from scratch every time it is initialized since we don't have a persistent DB with peer details (i.e. peer ID, chain ID, genesis hash, supported sub-protocols, latest hash/difficulty)

### How can it be fixed

We need to keep peer details/reputation in a persistent store so that the `PeerPool` can reuse known good peers from previous runs as well as provide APIs for clients to pick the _best_ peer for a request.

I'm not sure how to define _best_ here, but I believe a weighted round-robin based on a peer's latency would be a good start. Although we may also need a way to restrict the choices only to those peers that are at least at a certain block height/td

issue_title: Error with ACE and --tsdb-stdout with long unparsable sentences
issue_body:
When ACE v0.9.24 is installed, pyDelphin defaults to using the --tsdb-stdout option in order to get more information out of ACE. NOTEs and other messages to stderr are printed as normal. When a long unparsable sentence causes a NOTE to be generated, this message may be flushed to stderr before the stdout line has finished being written. Because pyDelphin interleaves stdout and stderr (in order to properly read the output; especially for generation), the NOTE on stderr makes the s-expression on stdout unreadable by pyDelphin.

Some solutions include:
- de-interleave stdout and stderr when `--tsdb-stdout` is used
- change ACE so it only flushes stderr after writing a newline to stdout
- load ACE for every input sentence so output can be read until EOF
- ignore stderr entirely when `--tsdb-stdout` is used

The example sentence is:

```
这个 学期 我 有 四 门 课 ， 不  太 忙 ， 我 每天 十点一 刻 去 上课 ， 十一点二十分 下课 ， 然后 ， 我 喜欢 去 图书馆 看书 ， 下午 下课 以后 ， 我 常 和 朋友 一起 去 打球 。
```

It was parsed using a recent version of [Zhong](https://github.com/delph-in/zhong) (the grammar at zhong/cmn/zhs/)

issue_title: conda install does not pick latest development builds from anaconda
issue_body:
Part of my workflow revolves around frequently uploading to anaconda and then downloading and installing development builds.

Following (at least, AFAICT) PEP440 I've starting naming these packages `<name>-X.Y-devN`.
Running  `conda install` on this package name without specifying version seems to pickup the older version (i.e the one with the lower value for N).

This is what the relevant section of meta.yaml file looks like (I was previously using only 'GIT_BUILD_STR' in the `string` section but removed it as PEP440 advises against using the commit sha):

```
package:
  name: <package-name>
  version: {{GIT_DESCRIBE_TAG}}

build:
  version: {{ environ.get('GIT_DESCRIBE_NUMBER', 0) }}
{% if environ.get('GIT_DESCRIBE_NUMBER', '0') == '0' %}
  string:
{% else %}
  string: dev{{ environ.get('GIT_DESCRIBE_NUMBER', '') }}
{% endif %}
```

The output of `conda info`

```
Current conda install:

               platform : linux-64
          conda version : 4.3.22
       conda is private : False
      conda-env version : 4.3.22
    conda-build version : 2.1.15
         python version : 3.5.3.final.0
       requests version : 2.14.2
```

issue_title: `conda install -f` implies `--no-deps` which is a problem for meta-packages
issue_body:
I believe this is related to #466 and #1255, but perhaps not.

First, it is unclear to me _why_ the `-f` option to `conda install` should imply `--no-deps`.  Certainly I would say that most people would not expect this to be the case.

But that issue aside, it is a problem for meta-packages which _only_ consist of dependencies.  If I do `conda install -f jupyter` then I want to force-install all the dependency packages specified the jupyter meta-package.  More than "I want", in fact "I expect" that is what will happen.  But it doesn't.  And I would suggest that the average user is not going to pick up on the visual hints that force-install of meta-packages do not install the dependencies.  They will just be confused that it hasn't happened or oblivious that it hasn't happened.

issue_title: Conda (small) bug under windows, searching for a badly named executable/script
issue_body:
With the install on Windows 10:

  _platform : win-64   conda version : 4.3.30
  conda is private : False   conda-env version : 4.3.30
  conda-build version : 3.0.22   python version : 3.6.3.final.0
  requests version : 2.18.4     root environment : C:\Anaconda3  (writable)_

the command:  `conda help install`  (or other similar, say, conda help create, etc.) produces the message

`C:\Anaconda3\python.exe: can't open file 'C:\Anaconda3\Scripts\conda': [Errno 2] No such file or directory`

This syntax works under Linux (Ubuntu), but of course, there is no just "conda" file in Windows Anaconda Scripts.

issue_title: conda install --revision x deleted rather than reverted
issue_body:
First time using revisions so may have misunderstood behaviour. However...

Wanted to undo an upgrade of a couple of packages (namely numpy and pandas). State at the start:

```
$ conda list --revisions
2017-01-11 13:23:00  (rev 0)
...
2017-03-07 10:52:22  (rev 3)
...
     numpy  {1.11.1 -> 1.12.0}
...
     pandas  {0.18.1 -> 0.19.2}
...
2017-05-03 14:27:31  (rev 5)
    +tqdm-4.11.2

2017-05-12 15:16:17  (rev 6)
     matplotlib  {2.0.0 -> 2.0.2}

2017-05-25 15:24:49  (rev 7)
     numpy  {1.12.0 -> 1.12.1}
     pandas  {0.19.2 -> 0.20.1}
```

so, I executed:

```
$ conda install --revision 6
Fetching package metadata ...........

Package plan for installation in environment /home/james/anaconda2/envs/mus:

The following packages will be REMOVED:

    numpy:  1.12.1-py27_0     
    pandas: 0.20.1-np112py27_0

Proceed ([y]/n)? y
```
I said yes to remove, as thought that would downgrade...but, state after:

```
$ conda list pandas
# packages in environment at /home/james/anaconda2/envs/mus:
#
$ conda list numpy
# packages in environment at /home/james/anaconda2/envs/mus:
#
gnumpy                    0.2                       <pip>
numpy                     1.12.0                    <pip>
numpydoc                  0.6.0                    py27_0  
$ conda list --revisions
2017-01-11 13:23:00  (rev 0)
...
2017-05-25 15:24:49  (rev 7)
     numpy  {1.12.0 -> 1.12.1}
     pandas  {0.19.2 -> 0.20.1}

2017-05-26 09:22:27  (rev 8)
    -numpy-1.12.1
    -pandas-0.20.1
```

You can see `pandas` and `numpy` are indeed removed and not, as I had expected, downgraded.

Is this expected behaviour?

conda info
===
```
$ conda info
Current conda install:

               platform : linux-64
          conda version : 4.3.18
       conda is private : False
      conda-env version : 4.3.18

issue_title: VCF v4.2 is not supported by pysam.cvcf
issue_body:
Hi,
I have been using pysam (release 0.8.4) to access VCF v4.1 files without any problems but I am not able to open a VCF v4.2.

I have replaced the header line "##fileformat=VCFv4.2" to "##fileformat=VCFv4.1", which allows the file to be opened, but then I get parsing errors from other header lines that are written in VCF v4.2 format.

So as far as I can tell, VCF v4.2 is not yet supported by pysam.

One of your developers told me about the API pysam.VariantFile. I tried to open and read a VCF v4.2 with the API and it did work.

However, I already have a pipeline implemented using pysam.cvcf and I would rather not to have to change it.

Is it possible to make pysam.cvcf work with VCF v4.2?

Another question, with this API will pysam.cvcf still be used or will it become obsolete?

Thanks,
Sofia

issue_title: AWS ES snapshot support
issue_body:
When using Curator with AWS Elasticsearch I can't take snapshots. It fails because because the endpoint `/_snapshot/_status` is not exposed by AWS. 

I believe this could be worked around by using `/_snapshot/<repository>/_status` instead, as that endpoint is exposed by AWS.

I'm not familiar with the Curator internals, so I'm not sure if this is a viable fix or not, but on the surface it seems that `/_snapshot/<repository>/_status` should provide the info required for Curator to take a snapshot.

issue_title: Resolve date math in dry run output
issue_body:
### Expected Behavior
When using date math for index names in dry run mode, the dry run output should render the index name with the date math applied.

### Actual Behavior
The logs include an "is using Elasticsearch date math" line:
```
2018-06-25 14:44:31,950 INFO               curator.utils     parse_date_pattern:1338 "<logstash-{now/d+1d}>" is using Elasticsearch date math.
```
and then display the "fully rendered name" as:
```
2018-06-25 14:44:31,951 DEBUG              curator.utils     parse_date_pattern:1349 Fully rendered name: <logstash-{now/d+1d}>
```

### Steps to Reproduce the Problem
```
actions:
  1:
    action: create_index
    description: >-
      Create index without shard replication for faster reindexing
    options:
      name: '<logstash-{now/d+1d}>'
      extra_settings:
        settings:
          number_of_shards: 1
          number_of_replicas: 0
      continue_if_exception: False
```

### Specifications

  - Version: 5.5

## Context (Environment)
It is difficult to verify that the provided date math is correct and valid because the dry run logging doesn't indicate what the final index name would be based on the date math.

## Detailed Description
Please render the full index name, including the applied date math, in the dry run logs.

issue_title: Simply the AZ login experience
issue_body:
The current experience for signing into Azure from the command line feels sluggish and requires multiple steps - here are some suggestions for simplifying this experience:

1.)  When you enter the command "az login" it takes several seconds without any indication that something is happening - at first, I wasn't sure it was working and I cancelled out of the command. To help with this, the command should provide some sort of output to the console to indicate that it's doing something.

2.) After entering the "az login" command, it eventually says "To sign in, use a web browser to open the page https://aka.ms/devicelogin and enter the code XXXXX to authenticate".  After the user enters this code into the browser, then they can log-in with their azure creds. This part of the experience could be improved by either allowing the creds to be entered directly in the command line without having the user go to the browser. Or, at least the browser could be automatically launched and the code already entered for them so that they don't have to manually launch the browser and enter in the code.

issue_title: VM: create LRO polling mechanism improvement
issue_body:
For now, we pull every 5 seconds.
We need to have this aggressive polling only for the first 400 seconds. After that we can have an incremental back-off. Having a flat polling every 5 seconds will have high risk of running into the ARM throttling limits.

issue_title: ResourceIdentity model class is missing
issue_body:
It's meant to be used in ResourceOperations methods, but not defined anywhere.
It looks like it's only used as input, so a workaround is to define it in your code:
```
class ResourceIdentity: 
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):

issue_title: Optimize conform CPU and disk IO
issue_body:
Now that the code is working and stable it'd be nice to spend some time optimizing performance. Some large sources like `nl` are timing out, and in general no effort has been made for efficiency. This issue is specifically about optimizing what happens after the source is downloaded. Some ideas:

[ ] Characterize performance of conform after the download is finished. Compare CSV vs. GeoJSON vs. Shapefile. Identify disk IO vs. CPU bottlenecks.
[ ] Experiment with psyco or PyPy and see if it helps. (Note: PyPy is mostly Py2 only.) Psyco uses a huge amount of RAM.
[ ] Consider removing the "extracted" intermediate CSV file; go straight from source material to output CSV in a row by row fashion. Should work conceptually, makes the code more complicated to debug. Only do it if disk IO is really a problem.
[ ] Random profiling / micro-optimizations in the inner loops.
[ ] I suspect the CSV DictReader is way inefficient for what we're doing, a simple indexed column thing might be better.

I'm happy to work on this when I'm back from vacation. Mostly opening the ticket to make some notes.

issue_title: Hinge2Vehicle wheels hanging - suspension problem
Contact point persists when objects are far apart.
issue_body:
I have a simple scene containing two boxes. I am looking at the contact points between them and I frequently see one lingering contact point despite the objects not being in contact.

![contact](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/120369/41111949-6794ef22-6a75-11e8-8067-e74f5269924a.gif)

I have been able to track this down to how contact points are reused. Initially, as the first box just begins to intersect the second, all contact points have normals pointing to the left since this is the direction of least penetration (all as expected).

Then, at a later point, the direction of least penetration changes so that all the normals should be pointing upwards. At this point, the insertion index for two of the contact points determined inside of `btManifoldResult::addContactPoint()` is -1 for two contacts because the minimum distance is greater than the contact breaking threshold. All fine so far.

For those two contact points we call `btPersistentManifold::addManifoldPoint()`, which obtains the insertion index from `btPersistentManifold::sortCachedPoints()`. This function retains the contact point with the greatest penetration depth. And herein lies the problem...

We retain one of the two contact points with the normals pointing to the left. This will from now on always have the greatest penetration distance, which will continue to grow in magnitude. The `btPersistentManifold::refreshContactPoints()` function will then never remove the contact point. The contact point only goes away when the broadphase determines that the two boxes are definitely not intersecting.

issue_title: New app signals proposal to track connections and requests
issue_body:
## Long story short

Right now there is not a set of signals in place to track connections and request for Aiohttp web server.  The proposal would implement the following subset of signals, attached to the application.

* `on_connection_created(app, context)` Triggered when a new connection has been created.
* `on_connection_closed(app, context)` Triggered when a connection has been closed.
* `on_request_start(app, context)` Triggered when a new request start.
* `on_request_end(app, context)` Triggered when a requests finishes.

All signals will receive as a parameter the app instance and a context that would help the user to pass information to the next semantic signal. For example, the `context` related with the connection will be instantiated when the `on_connection_created` is called and the value will be passed as a parameter for both connections methods, having the same for the request flavor.

These signals will be only triggered by the root app.

Taking into account that already exists a group of signals for the App and these differ a bit of the proposed ones, lack of context and so on. We could discuss on have a new module called `web_tracing` that will implement the same pattern as the client tracing [1], this might imply some name changing.

My 2cent about my cons with other ways to implement that:

* Middlewares are chained between the root app and nested apps, implement the request signals implicitly as a middleware IMHO is prone error and weak. Better having an ad-hoc set of signals that are never triggered by the nested apps.
* I would prefer to implement these signals out of the app scope, perhaps having them as a new parameter of the `run_app` that implements only these new signals. The problem with this solution is the incompatibility with environments that use the workers strategy to start the application such as `gunicorn`.

[1] https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/tracing.py

issue_title: ClientSession inside an aiohttp Server does not support connection pooling
issue_body:
I've got a aiohttp server that makes multiple http requests to other services in response to its inbound requests, I want to use a ClientSession for each inbound request to handle any cookie state that may be needed across the downstream requests it makes - but this means my server makes no use of connection-pooling/keep-alive when making downstream requests.

I could share a single ClientSession across all inbound requests, but then cookie/header state within the ClientSession would be re-used across inbound requests, which would not be correct. I could perhaps create a SharedClientSession object that handled switching between cookies/headers with a single underlying ClientSession, but that seems like I'm fighting the system.

Is there a supported way to do this? The most elegant way I can imagine would be a single ClientSession per inbound request with a global tcpconnector - except the ClientSession takes ownership and closes it, making it impossible to use in this way.

issue_title: Head doesn't follow redirects by default but get does
issue_body:
## Long story short

The documentation says that the default behaviour of the library when fetching URLs is to follow redirects (up to the defined redirect maximum). This doesn't seem to be the case - it is true if the fetch is a GET but it is not true if the fetch is a HEAD.

This is important because it isn't clear in the documentation that HEAD behaves differently. If it should behave differently, the documentation needs improving. If HEAD should behave the same as GET with regards to following redirects, this is a bug.

## Expected behaviour

Trying to GET a URL that returns a redirect follows that redirect. Trying to HEAD that same URL should also follow the redirect.

## Actual behaviour

Unless `allow_redirects=True` is explicitly specified, HEAD returns the redirect status rather than the status of following the redirect.

## Steps to reproduce

    import aiohttp
    import asyncio


    async def fetch(session, url):
        async with session.head(url) as response:
            return response.status, response.url


    async def fetch_all(session, urls, loop):
        results = await asyncio.gather(
            *[fetch(session, url) for url in urls],
        )

        for ret in results:
            print("status: %s url: %s" % (ret[0], ret[1]))
        return results

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        urls = ['http://www.linaro.org/this-doesnt-exist']
        with aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=loop) as session:
            loop.run_until_complete(fetch_all(session, urls, loop))

## Your environment

aiohttp (client) - 2.3.9
OS - Ubuntu 16.04
No proxy server

issue_title: Graceful shutdowns via Scheduler.wait()
issue_body:
I'm writing an app that requires a few background jobs to fetch requests from a queue and process them _atomically_.

When the main app receives a shutdown signal, I'm firing an `asyncio.Event` to let the jobs know they need to close after they finish processing the task, and I then `Job.wait()` each of them.

It would be more convenient to do this via the `Scheduler` class. Currently it only has support for `close()`ing all the jobs, but not for `wait()`ing them. Does it make sense to add such a function? A simplified version would look like this:

```python
class Scheduler:
    async def wait(self):
        await asyncio.gather(*[job.wait() for job in jobs])
```

issue_title: [Feature Request] Warn when opening same notebook twice
issue_body:
When you (usually, by accident) open the same notebook twice, an awkward situation may arise where changes can get lost. That is why an editor like `vim` will warn you when you open a file for editing that is already open in another session.

Would it be possible to add such a warning mechanism to the Jupyter Notebook Editor?

The **Notebook changed** warning (with Reload/Cancel/Overwrite options) is helpful, but it may come too late. Once, you end up in that situation, you may already have a merge problem for the changes made in the two edit sessions. It would be better to warn earlier, to prevent this situation.

issue_title: [Feature request] Each cell keeps track of execution time
issue_body:
Where ever possible, cells can keep track of execution time (say to the nearest microsecond). I don't want anything complex like `%time` or `%timeit, but it would be neat if each cell also stored when execution began, and when ended. Basically, in a `.ipnb` file, add key : value to store start and end times, and display it when mouse hovers over the cell execution count. Something similar to this:

```
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 3,
   "metadata": {
    "collapsed": false,
    "scrolled": true,
    "start_exec": <start datetime>
    "end_exec": <end datetime>
   },
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "print("foo")",
    "print("bar")"
   ]
  },
```

When displaying, it could subtract end time with start time, and also show the execution time for the cell. For advanced and accurate run timing, `%time` will be available. Therefore, this will be a crude or approximate time keeping.

This is useful to know how long a cell run roughly took to execute. I process billions of records on a daily basis, developing the code using Jupyter, and then moving it to a Python script for headless execution. Usually while developing in Jupyter, I work with a very small subset of the data, and often times try to keep a track of run times, so that I get an idea of how long the whole dataset will take. Even when I do, I am only interested in the nearest minute or second, because when my cells run for over 10 minutes, microsecond or nanosecond accuracy is immaterial.

My current approach is to note down the time when I press `Ctrl+Enter` and then stare at the screen till the run counter changes from `*` to a number, and note the time again. For a long run, for which I don't know when it might end, it is not a feasible method. Therefore, if the start time was set to when execute instruction is detected, and end time is set to when the counter number is incremented, and these times displayed when mouse hovers over the run counter (along with the difference between the two), it would be very helpful. Thanks.

issue_title: check parameters
issue_body:
libindy is checking parameters when called through the API.

Additionally there are check at other places like e.g.
https://github.com/hyperledger/indy-sdk/blob/master/libindy/src/commands/non_secrets.rs#L241
The 'from_json' call just checks whether the options string is deserializable but does not use the resulting object. Using the RecordOptions object does not really make sense because in the end the options end up in the wallet_storage API which requires a string anyway.

'fail-fast' is good but this looks like wasted CPU cycles. Not sure what to do about this. 

One line above the RecordOptions wasted json deserialization is a call to _check_type which makes it clearer what the purpose is: check_type is checking the type and RecordOptions::from_json is checking the options string.

Should those checks be configured away when a release version of libindy is build?
Should those checks be left to the code that is actually consuming the parameters, so fail-late?

issue_title: the design seems to be backward the django-rest-framework serializers.
issue_body:
I go throught some features of the both. and below was my opinion:

1) the validate raise error is not good. the django-rest-serializers return true or false, and store the errors in the models. this is more usable.

2) cannot serializers more than one field. the django-rest-serializers can use like below:
```python
users = UserModel([{'uid': '111', 'mobile': '111'}, {'uid': '222', 'mobile': '222'}], many=True)
```
but the schematics cannot do. indeed, many ORM-lib will support both single-and-many objects. for example: the mongoengine with queryset.

3) the serializable and roles design was really wonderful, anyway.

issue_title: Return index of invalid objects
issue_body:
So, in my models I define a `user`, and a `user` has many `addresses`, which are defined in their own class. So when mapping the model to a schematics class, it becomes something like this

```
class UserResource(BaseSchema):

    """User resource."""

    __entity__ = User
    user_addresses = ListType(ModelType(UserAddressResource))
```

Here's the problem: imagine I have a user with 5 addresses, but 2 of them are invalid. Currently, schematics returns something like `UserAddresses: [{'street': ['Not a well formed street name.']}, {'street': ['Not a well formed street name.']}]`.

It would be great to have the index of those invalid objects in the list. My only current option is to run the validation again on each object until I find the invalid ones. But this is not practical, considering it will be doing the same work twice, and models usually have more than one relationship, so it's linked to many other models.

issue_title: Potentially Wrong FFT gradients.
issue_body:
The implementation of FFT seems to be incorrect (in fft.py). At face value, it should have used the transposed matrix which are the inverse transforms, but I may have misunderstood the way dot-gradient operators are implemented in autograd.

Usually if the cost function is written with paired fourier transforms and inverse transforms, or if the cost function consists symmetric sums of fourier modes, the problem doesn't show up -- because the conjugate cancels itself.

I was able to construct the following example that gives inconsistent results between autograd and approx_fprime.  (autograd version is current on pip - 1.1.7.) 

```
a = numpy.random.normal(size=(4, 4))
r1 = fft.fftn(a).ravel()
mask = numpy.zeros_like(r1)
mask[:len(mask) //2] = 1.0

def func(x):
    r1 = fft.fftn(x
    r2 = r1 * mask.reshape(r1.shape)
    u = (abs(r2)**2)
    return u.sum()
dfunc = autograd.grad(func)

approx_fprime(a.ravel(), func, 1e-6)
# array([ 22.61698089, -12.77787632,  36.08745129,  27.8211549 ,
         8.45708857,  12.59130116, -38.21352857,  -5.92008325,
         4.91896941,  -2.05895782,  -1.42407114,   6.84178309,
         9.64427653,   9.20651456,  -8.18183236,  -1.79850173])
dfunc(a)
# array([[ 28.14276216,  -6.83041689,   9.36486957,  19.01041399],
       [ 18.70016245,   0.22143568, -24.40673976,   6.45993731],
       [ 10.44475068,   3.88850163, -28.14665287,  -1.96895785],
       [ 19.88735039,  -3.16335093,   5.62495646,  10.58151883]])
```

(ccing @rgiordan, since we talked about this yesterday).

Is this really a bug or we are not using autograd correctly?

issue_title: Version bump/pypi release at next merge to master
Difficulties in creating an ECDSA key
issue_body:
I know PyKMIP doesn't support the "Cryptographic Domain Parameters" attribute which is used to send the preferred curve. But that is ok because (AIUI) in KMIP it is acceptable when creating an ECDSA key to just specify "Cryptographic Length" and allow the server to pick a curve of its preference. However that does not work because `_build_key_attributes` in `kmip/pie/client.py` adds ENCRYPT/DECRYPT params as default and included in the common ops:

```
        # Default crypto usage mask value
        mask_value = [enums.CryptographicUsageMask.ENCRYPT,
                      enums.CryptographicUsageMask.DECRYPT]
        if masks:
            mask_value.extend(masks)
```

In ECDSA case of course this is just invalid since ECDSA cannot encrypt, so the request is rejected. (Well, it is not really clear from KMIP spec what the server should do in the event of invalid usage mask, but rejecting the request seems not unreasonable.) It seems to me that if the user specified `public_usage_mask` and `private_usage_mask` then it is not right to also silently enable encrypt/decrypt.

I'm doing this:

```
    key_id = client.create_key_pair(                                                                                                                                                           
        enums.CryptographicAlgorithm.ECDSA,                                                                                                                                                    
        256,                                                                                                                                                                                   
        operation_policy_name='default',                                                                                                                                                       
        public_name='Test_ECDSA1_pub',                                                                                                                                                         
        public_usage_mask=[                                                                                                                                                                    
            enums.CryptographicUsageMask.VERIFY                                                                                                                                                
        ],                                                                                                                                                                                     
        private_name='Test_ECDSA1',                                                                                                                                                            
        private_usage_mask=[                                                                                                                                                                   
            enums.CryptographicUsageMask.SIGN                                                                                                                                                  
        ]                                                                                                                                                                                      
    )                                                                                                                                                                                          
```

issue_title: Flush unitdata after every handler?
issue_body:
I've noticed during developing that if any handler fails, none of the unitdata gets flushed.  This means that on subsequent runs, states are reset to what they were at the start of the hook and even handlers that completed successfully are re-run.

I think we should flush after every successful handler to ensure that successful handlers are not re-run.  (Also note that everything is flushed before external handlers are run to ensure that they see the proper state.)

issue_title: Customize how entity objects are stringified
issue_body:
When a [`nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity`](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity) object is stringified, little useful information is provided. The full dotted path to the object's type is provided, in addition to its memory address. However, as is clear from the example below, that's not terribly useful, and it's ugly to boot:

```python
>>> location = Location().create()
>>> location.get_values()
{
    'compute_resource': [],
    'config_template': [<nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c0be90>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c0b910>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16050>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16310>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c165d0>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16890>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16b50>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16e10>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19110>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c193d0>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19690>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19950>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19c10>],
    'description': None,
    'domain': [],
    'environment': [],
    'hostgroup': [],
    'id': 18,
    'media': [],
    'name': u'\ufc8d\u1c73\uc3a5\u0564\ud17e\u7f1a\u55a2\ub819\u040f\uc88b\u569f\u478c\uadc5\uc8c0\ubeb7\u1565\u13c4\ucd9f\u588d\u4258\u42d3\ub4f5\u82e9\u3153\ub04b',
    'organization': [],
    'smart_proxy': [],
    'subnet': [],
    'user': [],
}
```

The process of stringifying entity objects should be customized. Something like this would be more useful:

    <ConfigTemplate 18>

This could backfire - not all entities have IDs. Perhaps this stringification method could try `self.id`, then `self.uuid`, then fall back to the memory address of the object?

issue_title: Drop the inner Meta classes on entities
issue_body:
The inner Meta classes are arguably a bad idea. A class should contain a bundle of data and logic that operates on that data. However, the Entity classes contain only data. By this reasoning, it is better to drop the Meta classes and add a regular class attribute to hold the same data.

I suggest creating a class attribute named `_meta`. I suggest that this class attribute be private for the following reasons:

* If `Meta`/`_meta` is changed, then *all* entity classes are affected. This is only something that should be done by someone who is an expert with NailGun and who really knows what they're doing.
* Experience with Robottelo shows that access to meta is unnecessary. The only modules that touch the current Meta class are `robottelo.entities` and `tests.foreman.api.test_multiple_path`. The former will soon be ported to NailGun, so those cases of access can be controlled. It is questionable whether the accesses to Meta in the latter case are really necessary.

This is a breaking change because the inner `Meta` class is a public attribute.

issue_title: Support per test capabilities
issue_body:
We are currently interested in using Zalenium and Browserstack to provide the Selenium grid which our tests can use. This brings us new requirements on how the browser capabilities are defined and the browser session lifecycle to enable us to set the test name and possibly also the success/failure status.

Rather than a single session scoped capability set, we need to have per test capabilities, parametrized with the test name.

Currently most fixtures have session scope and are used to build another session-scoped ``browser_instance_getter`` fixture that is then used to instantiate the test-scoped browser session, effectively capturing the browser kwargs and the capabilities in the session scope. So a wide refactoring seems to be needed to change this.

While we are at it it would be a plus to provide also some execution hook before the session is closed that could mark the test status (via setting a special cookie to Zalenium).

I am somewhat interested in developing this myself, but I feel it would need quite a change of the codebase and I am not sure how to tackle it without breaking too much.

issue_title: Command line arguments for plugins are broken
issue_body:
It seems the command line arguments for plugins created in ```add_parser_arguments(cls, add):``` cant be extracted with ```self.conf("argument_name")```. I suspect something with the plugin namespace prefix is going wrong.

My code is here https://github.com/e00E/lets-encrypt-icecast/blob/master/icecast.py .
In line 129 I add an argument via ```add("configuration_file")```
and I try to use it in line 150 like so
```
self.config_file = self.conf("configuration_file")
if self.config_file is not None and not os.path.isfile(self.config_file):
    raise PluginError('User supplied icecast configuration file does not exist.')
```

When running ```letsencrypt install``` it crashes with the last log message
```

issue_title: Recover registration URI
issue_body:
As per [ACME spec](https://letsencrypt.github.io/acme-spec/#registration):

"If the server already has a registration object with the provided account key, then it MUST return a 409 (Conflict) response and provide the URI of that registration in a Location header field. This allows a client that has an account key but not the corresponding registration URI to recover the registration URI."

This is not implemented by the ACME module

issue_title: How to suppress librdkafka log-messages completely and efficient ?
issue_body:
Description
===========
Hello,

i got some questions about suppressing log messages caused by librdkafka, please follow along the events:

Like in  @https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/issues/437 i see log messages from underlying librdkafka:

`1507111970.888|ERROR|rdkafka#consumer-11| <some_IP>:9092/49: Receive failed: Disconnected`

which i successfully suppress by using `'log.connection.close': 'false'`. Shortly after the above occures i see messages like these in my logs:

`1507111970.888|ERROR|rdkafka#consumer-11| 4/4 brokers are down`

As far as i understood this is a logical consequence. Since the producer is out of messages, let´s say "drained" it will loosely disconnect the consumers and notice them if there are new messages to consume.

Since the message is not important to me, i tried to suppress them like @https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/issues/915 suggests by setting `topic.metadata.refresh.interval.ms` to an interval lower than `connection.max.idle.ms`. Which i suppose (i do not have access to the Kafka instance nor configs) is about `300000 ms` .

This only does the job half-way because these messages still pop up every now and then. Also i wonder if this is the best way to solve the problem. Like @https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/issues/437#issuecomment-170458437 states, heartbeating is a costly thing.

Is there a better way to handle this behaviour which suppresses the messages and also is not that costly ?


How to reproduce
================

N/A

Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`('0.11.0', 720896)` and `('0.11.0', 721151)`):
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version: 0.9
 - [x] Client configuration: `{'session.timeout.ms': 6000,
        'log.connection.close': 'false',
        'api.version.request': 'false',
        'broker.version.fallback':'0.9.0.1', 
        'topic.metadata.refresh.interval.ms': 270000, 
        'default.topic.config':
            {'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest',
             'enable.auto.commit': 'true',
             'offset.store.method': 'broker'}}`
 - [x] Operating system: Linux
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [x] Provide broker log excerpts: N/A
 - [ ] Critical issue

issue_title: Feature request: pause/resume support
issue_body:
Hi all,
I've got a question about the difference in behaviour of consumer.poll(0).
If you check the java doc for the consumer ( [https://kafka.apache.org/0100/javadoc/index.html?org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.html](url)) it states the following for the poll(0) function:

> If 0, returns immediately with any records that are available currently in the buffer, else returns empty

I tried to do the same in python but when executing the code it already gives me the next record. 
Sample of the code I am using:
`

                        future_response= executor.submit(do_processing,msg.value(),self.logger, kafka_key.generated_uuid,False,False)

                        is_running = True
                        while is_running == True:
                            is_running=future_response.running()
                            self.logger.info("Still waiting for the future to finish. Going into sleep")
                            msgx=consumer.poll(0)
                            time.sleep(0.5)
`
If I do a print statement of msgx.value() in the while loop it gives me the next record. 
Why is there a difference in implementation ? 
Is there a way to do manual heartbeating in python?

Since the future might take a while to get the result I want to avoid to set session.timeout.ms to a very high value.

issue_title: Parser quirks in ISO8601 formats
issue_body:
A few issues I have with 3e76cb6b and ISO8601 formats:

* Parsing "2009-05" gives me: datetime.datetime(2009, 5, 24, 0, 0) which seems wrong. I'd expect datetime.datetime(2009, 5, 1, 0, 0)
* Parsing "2007-04-05T24:00" fails but I think this is a valid format.
* Parsing "2010-02-18T16.2" fails but I think this is a valid format.

I suggest these are added to the automatic tests. Most of these cases come from this blog post [https://www.myintervals.com/blog/2009/05/20/iso-8601-date-validation-that-doesnt-suck/] and it would be worth considering testing for as many as possible (I have not checked dateutil comprehensively against them).

issue_title: zoneinfo.gettz() objects are singletons for some reason
issue_body:
When exploring why the tests were failing on #225, I discovered that `tzfile()` objects constructed through `zoneinfo.gettz()` are completely immune to being copied - not entirely sure why:

```python
import copy
import dateutil
from dateutil import tz
from dateutil.zoneinfo import gettz

zone = 'Australia/Canberra'
z_t = tz.gettz(zone)
z_z = gettz(zone)

t0_t = z_t is z_t                           # True
t0_z = z_z is z_z                           # True

t1_t = z_t is tz.gettz(zone)                # False
t1_z = z_z is gettz(zone)                   # True (fair enough)

t2_t = z_t is copy.copy(z_t)                # False
t2_z = z_z is copy.copy(z_z)                # True (Curious...)

t3_t = z_t is copy.deepcopy(z_t)            # False
t3_z = z_z is copy.deepcopy(z_z)            # True

print("Dateutil version: {}".format(dateutil.__version__))     # 2.5.1
for tt, tz, st in [(t0_t, t0_z, 'same object: {}'),
                   (t1_t, t1_z, 'new construction: {}'),
                   (t2_t, t2_z, 'shallow copy: {}'),
                   (t3_t, t3_z, 'deep copy: {}')]:
    print("tz.gettz(), " + st.format(tt))
    print("zoneinfo.gettz(), " + st.format(tz))
```

This will probably need to be addressed as part of #225.

issue_title: Add isoparse() function.
issue_body: There is currently no inverse to the `isoformat()` function. If you *know* you've got an iso8601 date, the logic is actually *much* simpler than the current parser logic, but because `isoformat()` outputs a format not compatible with even Python 3.6's `strptime()` (shamefully), it's some pretty low hanging fruit.

                content = '<{}> {}'.format(msg1.media.__class__.__name__, getattr(msg1.media, 'caption', ''))
            elif hasattr(msg. 'message'):
                content = msg.message
            elif hasattr(msg, 'action'):
                content = str(msg.action)
            else:
                content = msg.__class__.__name__
            self.invoke(ForwardMessageRequest(get_input_peer(entities[2]), msg.id, generate_random_long()))

issue_title: Separate panel for pending events
issue_body: A separate panel/page should be added to the events section of the manager to provide an interface for approving pending events. This page should show a list of events that have `is_submission=True` by using the `pending=1` flag in the API call (see #562). After clicking on an event, the user should be taken to a page that displays the event information along with an "Approve" and "Disapprove" button. Clicking "Approve" should update the `is_submission` flag via an API call and then redirect the user back to the pending events page. Clicking the "Disapprove" button should delete the event and return the user to the pending events page.

issue_title: Nested schemas requires all validations to pass
issue_body:
If the validation of a single field in a nested schema fails, then values of the entire nested schema are thrown away. Example:

~~~
import marshmallow as ma

class Child(ma. Schema):
    a = ma.fields.Integer()
    b = ma.fields.Integer()

class Parent(ma.Schema):
    x = ma.fields.Integer()
    y = ma.fields.Integer()
    z = ma.fields.Nested(Child)

p = Parent()
~~~

If I call p.load() with a dict that has 'x' set to a valid integer, and 'y' set to None, the returned data has one value, and the error list contains on error message for 'y'

But if I do the same thing with the child instance ('z') and set for example 'a' to None, and 'b' to a valid value, then the entire nested schema ('z') is absent from the data returned by p.load()

I'm trying to use marshmallow for saving settings to disk. I do *not* want the loading method to skip an entire nested object just because one of the values of it are invalid.

Any ideas?

issue_title: RFC: replace load_from and dump_to with a single parameter
issue_body:
See discussion in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/714.

In the general case, dump/load is symmetric and if you have a model field that is exposed with another name, you use the API name in the schema, and specify the model name as `attribute`.

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             ApiName = fields.String(attribute='model_name')
```

`dump_to` and `load_from` introduce asymmetry, allowing to specify a different key for dump and load:

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             model_name = fields.String(load_from='api_load_name', dump_to='api_dump_name')
```

If you want to reproduce use case 1 using these, you need to specify both `load_from` and `dump_to` and make sure they match:

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             model_name = fields.String(load_from='api_name', dump_to='api_name')
```

The flexibility brought by having both `load_from` and `dump_to` comes at a price: complexity for the user but also in the code (marshmallow and related libs like webargs, apispec...), potential undefined cases for some weird combinations...

Assuming symmetry is not required, a single parameter should be enough. There is however a limitation with `attribute`. It can't be used for APIs where the keys are invalid Python variable names:

```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    ApiKey = fields.String(attribute='apikey')
    weird-key = fields.String(attribute='weird_key')  # syntax error
```

Currently, `load_from` and `dump_to` can be used for this as shown above.

The proposal here is to introduce a new parameter to unite them all. Let's call it `key` for now, short of a better name:

```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    apikey = fields.Int(key='ApiKey')
    weird_key = fields.Int(key='weird-key')
```

If we do that, then there is no point in `attribute` anymore (except backward compatibility). So we'd remove `attribute`, `dump_to` and `load_from`.

Any objection to this?

Any suggestion for a better name?

lookup_kwargs = dict((k, v) for k, v in data.iteritems() if getattr(fk_resource, k).unique)

to:

lookup_kwargs = dict((k, v) for k, v in data.iteritems() if k in fk_resource.fields and getattr(fk_resource, k).unique)

issue_title: If a dataset is transformed to another Dataset with more elements, the opimimiter cannot infer the correct number of elements in each Epoch.
issue_body:
If the user create an Distributed DataSet and then use transformers to transform the rdd to a rdd with more elements, then put the DataSet to a ptimizer, the optimizer can not automatically inter the correct number of elements in each epoch. Here a simple example is given as follows:

```scala
case class Tokenizer(sep: String) extends Transformer[String, Seq[String]] 

// flatten the given iterator
case class Flatten() extends Transformer[Seq[String], String] {
  override def apply(prev: Iterator[Seq[String]]): Iterator[String] =
    prev.flatten
}

val trainSet = (DataSet.rdd(sc.textFile(params.trainDataLocation)) 
     -> Tokenizer() 
     -> Flatten() 
     -> ToBatch(batchSize))

val optimizer = Optimizer(
      model = model,
      dataset = trainSet,
      criterion =  ... )
```

In the above case, assume the lines in the given input text file is 100, then it was transformed into 1000 words. However, during the training process, the optimizer still regards the size of training set is 100 in each epoch. It is better to enhance the function to meet this requirement.

For now, the solution can be that:
1. Transform the DataSet to RDD and then map the transformer
2.  Transform the result RDD to DataSet and then transform to Batch.

code example as follows:
```scala
val trainSetRDD = DataSet.rdd(sc.textFile(params.trainDataLocation)
      .toDistributed()
      .data(false)
      .mapPartitions[String]((Tokenizer() -> Flatten()).apply)

val trainSet = DataSet.rdd(trainSetRDD) -> ToBatch(batchSize)
```
