issue_title: find new turbidity data
issue_body:
Has anybody ever looked at the default turbidity data? I finally got around to it and I think it's horrible. 

First, I was surprised when changing my latitude and longitude by just a small amount gave me fairly different results.

``` python
times = pd.DatetimeIndex(start='2015-01-01', end='2016-01-01', freq='1D')
pvlib.clearsky.lookup_linke_turbidity(times, 32, -111, interp_turbidity=False).plot(label='Tucson1')
pvlib.clearsky.lookup_linke_turbidity(times, 32.2, -110.9, interp_turbidity=False).plot(label='Tucson2')
pvlib.clearsky.lookup_linke_turbidity(times, 33.5, -112.1, interp_turbidity=False).plot(label='Phoenix')
pvlib.clearsky.lookup_linke_turbidity(times, 35.1, -106.6, interp_turbidity=False).plot(label='Albuquerque')
plt.legend()
```

![monthly](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4383303/11155186/49466eda-8a01-11e5-98e4-3473bf63855b.png)

The values for Tucson change by up to 0.5 depending on if I round to the nearest degree. No significant terrain differences between these points. 

Then I decided to just plot the full array for every month. Here's the code plus a couple of images.

``` python
import pvlib
import calendar
import os
import scipy.io

pvlib_path = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(pvlib.clearsky.__file__))
filepath = os.path.join(pvlib_path, 'data', 'LinkeTurbidities.mat')

mat = scipy.io.loadmat(filepath)
linke_turbidity_table = mat['LinkeTurbidity'] / 20

for month in range(0, 12):
    plt.figure(figsize=(20,10))
    plt.imshow(linke_turbidity_table[:,:,month], vmin=1, vmax=5, )
    plt.title(calendar.month_name[1+month])
    plt.colorbar()
```

![january](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4383303/11155160/21d155b8-8a01-11e5-90e2-d9276c907d8d.png)
![july](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4383303/11155161/21efb378-8a01-11e5-812d-855b044c2473.png)

Sure, it has the global, seasonal trends right, but there is a ton of crap in there.

issue_title: [proposal] allow user to specify the scaling of singlediode ivcurve-pnts or a specify an array, series, or sequence
issue_body:
This idea also came up somewhere in #409 or #410. Currently the `singlediode` `ivcurve_pnts` takes the following according to docstring:
>ivcurve_pnts : None or int, default None
    Number of points in the desired IV curve. If None or 0, no
    IV curves will be produced.

### idea 1:
In addition let the user specify a sequence. Should it be sorted? Which values do they correspond to, I, V or Vd? IMO we should sort and scale them to the max value, and then multiply by Voc to keep them in the 1st quadrant.

### idea 2:
Add another argument like `ivcurve_scale` which could be `'log'` or `'linear'` or other? IMO the default should be a log scale

Other ideas? Yea or Nay?

issue_title: ResourceRelatedField: Support resource_name in to_internal_value
issue_body:
I'm trying to modify the `type` of a `ResourceRelatedField`: Instead of the model name, i'd like to serve another name. 

For `GET` requests, that works nicely because `to_representation` considers `resource_name` on the serializer (if the field is part of `included_serializers`).

However, `POST`ing a new resource under the different `resource_name` does not work, because `to_internal_value` considers only the model for `expected_relation_type`.

**Suggestion**: Allow passing `resource_name` to `ResourceRelatedField`. If specified, it 

- is used in `to_internal_value` to map to the actual resource name (which is read from the queryset)
- should take priority over `resource_name` on the serializer in `to_representation`

What do you think? Is there an easier approach to this? I'd be happy to provide a PR if you think this is the way to go.

PS: In case you wonder, our use case for this: We need two different endpoints with different permissions and serializers for the same underlying Django model. (The "owner" of the model sees more than anyone else.)

issue_title: Keras progress bar doesn't look right since keras update
issue_body:
Since Keras has updated, adding `ex.captured_out_filter = apply_backspaces_and_linefeeds` no longer helps sacred to override the repeated lines with FileObserver, making the logs extremely long. This effect also happens in the terminal from which the script was called.

An [issue](https://github.com/fchollet/keras/issues/8414) has been opened at keras repository, but a contributor suggests this is a sacred problem.

**Steps to reproduce**

`progbar_test.py`:
```python
import keras
from keras import callbacks
from keras.datasets import mnist
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dense
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sacred import Experiment

ex = Experiment('training-a-keras-model')

@ex.config
def my_config():
    batch_size = 128
    num_classes = 10
    epochs = 20

@ex.automain
def main(batch_size, num_classes, epoch):
    (x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = mnist.load_data()
    x_train = x_train.reshape(60000, 784).astype('float32') / 255
    x_test = x_test.reshape(10000, 784).astype('float32') / 255
    y_train = keras.utils.to_categorical(y_train, num_classes)
    y_test = keras.utils.to_categorical(y_test, num_classes)

    model = Sequential([
      Dense(1024, activation='relu', name='fc1', input_dim=784),
      Dense(1024, activation='relu', name='fc3'),
      Dense(num_classes, activation='softmax', name='predictions')
    ])
    model.compile(optimizer='adam',
                  loss='categorical_crossentropy',
                  metrics=['accuracy'])

    history = model.fit(x_train, y_train,
                        batch_size=batch_size,
                        epochs=epochs,
                        verbose=1,
                        validation_data=(x_test, y_test))
```

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'debug'
```

It seems to me that the experiment must be running to call captured functions.
I guess a simple fix to this problem would be to change `sacred/config/captured_function.py`:
```
@wrapt.decorator
def captured_function(wrapped, instance, args, kwargs):
    options = FallbackDict(
        wrapped.config,
        _config=wrapped.config,
        _log=wrapped.logger,
        _run=wrapped.run
    )
    if wrapped.uses_randomness:  # only generate _seed and _rnd if needed
        options['_seed'] = get_seed(wrapped.rnd)
        options['_rnd'] = create_rnd(options['_seed'])

    bound = (instance is not None)
    args, kwargs = wrapped.signature.construct_arguments(args, kwargs, options,
                                                         bound)
    if wrapped.logger is not None:
        wrapped.logger.debug("Started")
        start_time = time.time()
    # =================== run actual function =================================
    result = wrapped(*args, **kwargs)
    # =========================================================================
    if wrapped.logger is not None:
        stop_time = time.time()
        elapsed_time = timedelta(seconds=round(stop_time - start_time))
        wrapped.logger.debug("Finished after %s.", elapsed_time)

    return result

```

A even nicer thing would be to have a sacred interface to MongoDB like this:

```
// wrapper of mongodb
db = SacredMongoDB()
db.find({
    'experiment.name': {'$eq': 'train_area_models_4x4'},
    'config.use_conditional': False,
})

# get one experiment
ex = next(db)

# access config
ex.config.use_conditional           # -> False

# access experiment values
ex.experiment.name                  # -> train_area_models_4x4

# call captured functions with the config from the database
ex.create_model()

# raises Exception. Experiment already run
ex.run()
```

I probably could create such a wrapper for mongodb if you think this is useful
to have for sacred.

issue_title: Sort error in proof
issue_body:
Consider the following SMT-LIB 2 problem:

```
(set-option :produce-proofs true)
(set-logic AUFLIA)
(declare-sort B$ 0)
(declare-sort A_set$ 0)
(declare-sort A_set_set$ 0)
(declare-sort A_set_set_set$ 0)
(declare-sort A_set_set_bool_fun$ 0)
(declare-fun a$ () B$)
(declare-fun uu$ (B$) A_set_set_bool_fun$)
(declare-fun uua$ () A_set_set_bool_fun$)
(declare-fun card$ (A_set$) Bool)
(declare-fun card$a (A_set_set_set$) Bool)
(declare-fun member$ (A_set$) A_set_set_bool_fun$)
(declare-fun collect$ (A_set_set_bool_fun$) A_set_set_set$)
(declare-fun fun_app$ (A_set_set_bool_fun$ A_set_set$) Bool)
(declare-fun partitions$ (A_set_set$ B$) Bool)
(assert (! (forall ((?v0 A_set_set$)) (! (= (fun_app$ uua$ ?v0) (and (partitions$ ?v0 a$) (forall ((?v1 A_set$)) (=> (fun_app$ (member$ ?v1) ?v0) (card$ ?v1))))) :pattern ((fun_app$ uua$ ?v0)))) :named a0))
(assert (! (forall ((?v0 B$) (?v1 A_set_set$)) (! (= (fun_app$ (uu$ ?v0) ?v1) (and (partitions$ ?v1 ?v0) (forall ((?v2 A_set$)) (=> (fun_app$ (member$ ?v2) ?v1) (card$ ?v2))))) :pattern ((fun_app$ (uu$ ?v0) ?v1)))) :named a1))
(assert (! (forall ((?v0 A_set_set_bool_fun$) (?v1 A_set_set_bool_fun$)) (=> (forall ((?v2 A_set_set$)) (= (fun_app$ ?v0 ?v2) (fun_app$ ?v1 ?v2))) (= (collect$ ?v0) (collect$ ?v1)))) :named a2))
(assert (! (forall ((?v0 B$)) (card$a (collect$ (uu$ ?v0)))) :named a3))
(assert (! (not (card$a (collect$ uua$))) :named a4))
(check-sat)
(get-proof)

```

The proof we obtain is sort-incorrect, whether we use an oldish version of Z3 from about one year ago or the latest repository version. In several places, the ill-sorted term
(fun_app$ (member$ ?v1) ?v1)
appears in it. However, ?v1 cannot be simultaneously of sort "A_set" and "A_set_set".

issue_title: fp.rem constant-folding float/double performance
issue_body:
I have 2 identical benchmarks for computing (i.e., constant-folding) `fp.rem NaN NaN`; one for single-precision and the other for double-precision.

Turns out z3 can answer the query on single-precision in about 9 seconds on my (somewhat dated) computer. Rather slow for constant folding; but perhaps acceptable given all the complexity associated with FPA.

But z3 simply does not complete the`Double` instance at all: I had to kill it about 5 mins into the run-time.

Both benchmarks are included below; first for `Float` and then for `Double`. Perhaps there's a performance issue around constant-folding with floats/doubles that really throw Z3 off in case of double-precision numbers?

`Float` version; z3 answers it in about 9 seconds on my computer:

```
; Automatically generated by SBV. Do not edit.
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
; --- uninterpreted sorts ---
; --- literal constants ---
(define-fun s_2 () Bool false)
(define-fun s_1 () Bool true)
; --- skolem constants ---
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint  8 24))
(declare-fun s1 () (_ FloatingPoint  8 24))
(declare-fun s2 () (_ FloatingPoint  8 24))
; --- constant tables ---
; --- skolemized tables ---
; --- arrays ---
; --- uninterpreted constants ---
; --- user given axioms ---
; --- formula ---
(assert ; no quantifiers
   (let ((s3 (fp.isNaN s0)))
   (let ((s4 (fp.isNaN s1)))
   (let ((s5 (fp.rem s0 s1)))
   (let ((s6 (= s2 s5)))
   (let ((s7 (and s4 s6)))
   (let ((s8 (and s3 s7)))
   s8)))))))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

```

issue_title: The combined solver, incremental solving, and activation literals
issue_body:
Our application uses Z3 and activation literals for incremental solving quite heavily.
We use a configuration with a combined solver, where the incremental solver (which lacks sophisticated preprocessing in its tactics) has a rather short timeout, and the non-incremental solver uses a much longer timeout.

We introduce activation literals not by using assert_and_track, but by asserting implications of the form
a_i => formula_i on the solver stack. Then check_sat is called incrementally with subsets of these a_i.

Against our expectations, we are ending up using the incremental solver without the desired short timeout being imposed by the combined_solver implementation. The fallback to the non-incremental solver does not occur. 

The reason seems to be that the code in https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/71fff8ffa2fa80a34d2b3bf3f705ef0852310b04/src/solver/combined_solver.cpp#L196-L202 shortcuts the entire combined_solver heuristic and simply applies the (often slow) incremental solver2.
I wonder why this is necessary. As far as I understood the Z3 sources, activation literals that are defined by asserting implications as sketched above, are held in sync between solver1 and solver2. 
So it should be possible to use the combined_solver heuristic for such queries, too.
The only thing one has to change is to propagate num_assumptions and assumptions into the check_sat calls for solver1 and solver2. Some experiments that I have run seem to support that claim.

Is there a good reason for always resorting to the incremental solver once activation literals are specified? Or is this only necessary for activation literals created via the assert_and_track method?
Could the combined_solver distinguish between these two scenarios?
This might also be related commit 9b137d54d34e0a014b4c5c5c9c21cbf43b78f604

issue_title: Feature Request: Start optimization from given model
issue_body: It would be nice to have a z3-opt API like `optimize(model_ref& base_model)`, which starts optimisation from the given base model. Currently, `optimize()` always calls into a fresh solver to get a solution on the hard constraints before starting the optimisation procedure, which can be wasteful in some situations.

issue_title: fp.min/fp.max/fp.neg Bug with +/- 0.0
issue_body:
On the `master` branch at commit `cef8c67cc0e6b1b024d8e4e0150b2020688de7c7` with a debug + tracing build, consider the following input:

```
(set-logic QF_FP)
(assert (= (_ -zero 2 3)
                (fp.max       ; Point 4
                  (fp.min     ; Point 3
                    (fp.neg   ; Point 2
                      (fp.min ; Point 1
                        (_ -zero 2 3)
                        (_ +zero 2 3)))
                    (_ +zero 2 3))
                  (fp #b1 #b10 #b01))))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

If this is saved into `test.smt2` and then run with `z3 -smt2 test.smt2`, Z3 reports `unsat`.  Tracing the logic of this, at `Point 1` +/- 0.0 is possible based on the reasoning in issue #68, and similarly both +/- 0.0 is possible at `Point 2`.  `Point 3` still allows +/- 0.0, either of which is greater than `(fp #b1 #b10 #b01)`, allowing `Point 4` to be +/- 0.0.  Following this reasoning, this input should be `sat` at the comparison to -0.0.

While it might be possible to minimize this further, I found that the `fp.neg` is significant, as are nested uses of `fp.min` and `fp.max`.

This might be a duplicate of the closed issue #68.  I'm reporting it separately here as opposed to reopening #68 as this issue seems intertwined with `fp.neg` in some way, which is distinct from #68.

issue_title: Auto-reload dev server on change
issue_body:
It would be really helpful if `<app> develop run` would auto reload when a change is made in a file.

Maybe this already exists?

issue_title: Safe to assume Blinker
issue_body: Right now, we are assuming a Keg app can run without Blinker.  However, since we are kicking off our testing prep with a signal, it's safe to assume Blinker will need to be installed.

issue_title: BaseView: assign multiple endpoints
issue_body: What if we wanted 'auth.after-reset' and 'auth.after-verify' to both go to a single endpoint?  I don't think BaseView has anything that would support this.

issue_title: Batch normalization - why not use theano?
issue_body: So, is there any reason why Lasagne has still not started using the `T.nnet.batch_normalization_train/test` for the `BatchNormalizationLayer`? I did a small test and with `fast_compile` the Lasagne implementation is very numerically unstable while the Theano works fine even with optimizer `none`.

issue_title: NaN Values creeping up on a simple CNN
issue_body:
So I spend my whole day trying to debug this, so hopefully someone can at least give me an advise. I get this NaN values on quite a simple CNN. The code is below:

```
import numpy as np
import theano.tensor as T
import theano
import lasagne
import time
from theano.compile.nanguardmode import NanGuardMode
from toppings.datasets import get_dataset
from toppings.transform import zero_mean, unit_var
from toppings.utils import print_epoch

DIM_N = 256
def main():
    var_in = theano.tensor.TensorType(theano.config.floatX, (False, )*4)("images")
    var_t = theano.tensor.TensorType(theano.config.floatX, (False, )*3)("targets")

    in_l = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((None, 9, DIM_N, DIM_N), name="images",input_var=var_in)
    cnn_l = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(in_l,num_filters=32,filter_size=3,pad="same",name="conv_11")
    pred_l = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(cnn_l,num_filters=1,filter_size=5,pad="same",
                                        nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.sigmoid,name="combine_4_org")

    cond_l = lasagne.layers.NonlinearityLayer(pred_l,nonlinearity=lambda x: T.clip(x,1e-5,1-1e-5), name="combine_4")
    r_l = lasagne.layers.ReshapeLayer(cond_l,(-1,DIM_N,DIM_N),name="outputs")
    target_l = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((None, DIM_N, DIM_N),name="targets",input_var=var_t)

    outputs = lasagne.layers.get_output(r_l)
    targets = lasagne.layers.get_output(target_l)
    params = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(r_l, trainable=True)
    loss = 0
    loss += lasagne.objectives.binary_crossentropy(outputs,targets).mean()
    reg_p = lasagne.layers.get_all_params(r_l, regularizable=True)
    loss += 0.0005 * lasagne.regularization.apply_penalty(reg_p,lasagne.regularization.l2)

    lr = theano.shared(np.array(0.001,dtype=theano.config.floatX))
    m = theano.shared(np.array(0.9,dtype=theano.config.floatX))
    updates = lasagne.updates.nesterov_momentum(loss, params, learning_rate=lr, momentum=m)

    loss_f = theano.function([in_l.input_var, target_l.input_var], loss, updates=updates,
                            mode=NanGuardMode(nan_is_error=True, inf_is_error=True, big_is_error=True))

    theano.printing.debugprint(loss_f, print_type=True)

    # Get data
   n = 128
   train_data = [np.random.randn(n,9,256,256).astype(theano.config.floatX),
                  np.random.randint(2,size=(n,256,256)).astype(theano.config.floatX)]
    print np.min(train_data[0])
    print np.max(train_data[0])
    print np.min(train_data[1])
    print np.max(train_data[1])

    bs = n / 32

    index = np.arange(n, dtype="int32")

    for epoch in range(10):
            # Shuffle if needed
            start_time = time.time()
            np.random.shuffle(index)

            # Iterate over mini batches
            loss = 0
            for i in range(0, n, 32):
                ind = index[i:i+32]
                data = [var[ind] for var in train_data]
                if i+32 > n:
                    m = float(n - i) / 32
                    loss += m * loss_f(*data)
                else:
                    loss += loss_f(*data)

            # Normalize the loss, save it and print
            error = [loss / bs]
            timing = time.time() - start_time
            print_epoch(timing, epoch+1, error,False)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

In short I get as an input stacked 3 images (9x256x256) and have a target a single grayscale image for segmentation (256x256). Since there are 2 classes I use binary cross entropy. I can't attach the data here, but I've tried to run this with some randomly generated. Thus when I run this code on the first epoch I get (sometimes second or third):

issue_title: Better prompt option, like $_ in cmd.exe
issue_body:
In Windows cmd.exe, by default the prompt is pushed far from the left edge, by display of the directory path. This is awkward and ugly to the eye, as commands of modest length must wrap to the next line.
Not enough people know about the $_ option a cmd.exe window (or in System Environment variables), as in:

> set Prompt=[$P\]$_$+$G$G$S

The above 'set' command changes the prompt from a single line to a double line, due to $_.  Compare the next two prompt examples (1 = single line, 2 = double line):

1.
C:\users\genemi\MyFunDirectory>echo Hello World


2.
[C:\users\genemi\MyFunDirectory\]
>> echo Hello World


So, please enable prompt configuration so commands can start near the left edge, while still displaying the database name or whatever in the preceding line.  :-) 

Thanks. GeneMi @ microsoft. com  (Tuesday  2017/12/12  18:44pm  Redmond time)

issue_title: run loops for just one iteration?
issue_body: Is there a way to have nbval-lax run slightly different code than the what Jupyter does? In the Jupyter notebook, I have an algorithm that runs for a long time -- too long for unit tests. I'd like to nbval-lax to run that loop for just 1 iteration. Is there a way to do that?

issue_title: Interaction with cell magics
issue_body:
Cell magic syntax requires that the cell magic is on the first line. This causes problems when that cell needs to be ignored by nbval, as nbval doesn't pick up the '#PYTEST_VALIDATE_IGNORE_OUTPUT' unless it's on the first line.

I'd suggest maybe the way to do this is to check whether the first line is a cell magic, and if so, check whether the following line in the cell is an nbval statement.

Dependency sinon

issue_title: Wrong encoding for reading notebooks on Windows
issue_body:
Hi,

we are unable to execute notebooks on Windows platform because the notebooks cannot be read due to incorrect file encoding. Notebooks are plain json files and as such encoded in UTF-8. However, the default encoding (on windows) is set according to the locale (in our case CP1250). Notebooks are read via `LocalHandler` in `iorw.py`. The function `io.open` is used, but no encoding specified and thus (incorrectly) set to CP1250. Would it be possible to fix this? There is the unfortunate problem of different approach in Python 2.7 and Python 3+ to handle file encoding. Thanks.

issue_title: CFGAccurate.remove_cycles() function never removes any cycles
issue_body:
Function CFGAccurate.remove_cycles() does not remove any cycles from CFG because of type mismatch. 
`cfg._loop_back_edges` returns edges with `Blocknode` types while the `cfg._graph` has `CFGnode` type. 

The `if self._graph.has_edge(b1, b2):` condition inside the function never becomes true even if the edge is present in the graph. So the loop back edge never gets removed from the graph

issue_title: about veritesting exploration tech
issue_body:
I read the original veritesting paper and angr implementation and found some (maybe bugs) ?:

1.the veritesting tech ignored some stashes, only the following stashes merged into main simgr:
https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/exploration_techniques/veritesting.py#L28
obviously the errored stash is discarded, this may cause some error state not detected by those checkers relying on error stash, maybe deadended and some others should be kept too and let higher level code to drop it or generate testcase?

2.the original paper do veritesting only at symbolic branch starting point, but from the exploration technique code, it seems that veritesting analysis is on at every state-step this may slow the SE noticeabley, maybe it's better to do at sym forking point by hooking 'engine_process'?

3. it seems https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/analyses/veritesting.py#L196 is not necessary because https://github.com/angr/angr/blob/master/angr/analyses/veritesting.py#L229 has already done this?

issue_title: Possibly argument of wrong type is used to initialize SimStruct
issue_body:
According to the implementation of `SimStruct`, it seems that the `fields` attribute is expected to be an instance of `dict`. However, in at least the `glibc.py` module, there are lots of `SimStruct`s that are initialized with fields argument of type `list`, for example:

```python
# struct group * getgrnam (const char *NAME);
"getgrnam": SimTypeFunction([SimTypePointer(SimTypeChar(label=None), label=None, offset=0)], SimTypePointer(SimStruct([], name="group", pack=True), label=None, offset=0), label=None),
```

This could lead to a confusing "Can't fit value into a return value" error:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
  File "/home/user/angr/angr/sim_procedure.py", line 310, in ret
    arg_types=[False]*self.num_args if self.cc.args is None else None)
  File "/home/user/angr/angr/calling_conventions.py", line 556, in teardown_callsite
    self.set_return_val(state, return_val)
  File "/home/user/angr/angr/calling_conventions.py", line 608, in set_return_val
    raise ValueError("Can't fit value %s into a return value" % repr(val))
ValueError: Can't fit value <BV32 unconstrained_ret_getgrnam_387_32{UNINITIALIZED}> into a return value
```

The real cause of that problem is that the `SimType.fields` was initialized with a `list` instead of `dict`, hence the following code threw an AttributeError:

```python
class SimStruct(SimType):
    # ...
    def _with_arch(self, arch):
        if arch.name in self._arch_memo:
            return self._arch_memo[arch.name]

        out = SimStruct(None, name=self.name, pack=self._pack, align=self._align)
        out._arch = arch
        self._arch_memo[arch.name] = out
        # Next line will raise AttributeError("'list' object has no attribute 'iteritems'") in case
        # the self.fields were initialized with 'list' instead of 'dict'
        out.fields = OrderedDict((k, v.with_arch(arch)) for k, v in self.fields.iteritems())
        return out
```

Which will be later caught by:

```python
class ArgSession(object):
    # ...
        try:
            dbg.settrace('unconstrained_ret_getgrnam_387_32' in val.variables)
            betterval = self._standardize_value(val, ty, state, None)
        except AttributeError:
            raise ValueError("Can't fit value %s into a return value" % repr(val))
```

To this time, I was unable to fix it by myself, 'cause I'm not familiar with SimType at all. Or am I missing something?

issue_title: Alternative binary keyvalue
issue_body:
The alternative format uses `0x0b` as END instead of `0x08`, otherwise it exactly the same. I have no idea why that is. Is it a programming typo, `0x0B` looks like `0x08`? Maybe it's supposed to be different, say format for on disk, rather than just data interchange over the steam network. Explains the CRC32, but why change the format?

Samples files:

- `0x08` - `[steamdir]/appcache/appinfo.vdf`
- `0x0B` - `[steamdir]/userdata/[account id]/570/remote/cfg/chat.cfg`
File starts with `VBKV`, followed by 4 bytes CRC32 of the rest, then binary vdf

issue_title: 分钟级别的回测事件源不准确
issue_body:
在simulation_event_source.py的_get_stock_trading_minutes函数中会生成分钟级别的事件源:
```
def _get_stock_trading_minutes(trading_date):
        trading_minutes = set()
        current_dt = datetime.datetime.combine(trading_date, datetime.time(9, 31))
        am_end_dt = current_dt.replace(hour=11, minute=30)
        pm_start_dt = current_dt.replace(hour=13, minute=1)
        pm_end_dt = current_dt.replace(hour=15, minute=0)
...
```
从上面的代码可以看出有如下问题:
1. 上午9:30分未生成事件, 而是在9:31分才开始生成事件, 这是不准确的,  其实在9:25分时已经生成了开盘价, 9:30分已经具备生成事件的所有信息, 应该生成交易事件.

2. 上午11:30分生成了事件, 但上午11:30分已近停牌了, 此时不应再发送事件了.

3. 同理, 下午13:00分应生成事件, 15:00分不应生成事件.

issue_title: BUG: erroneous detail coefficients returned by downcoef with level > 1
issue_body:
As pointed out by @jairongfu in #72, it seems that there is still a problem for ``downcoef`` with detail coefficients and level > 1.  Unfortunately, the tests i added in #72 only tested the approximation case!

example reproducing the problem:
```python
import pywt
import numpy as np
from numpy.testing import assert_array_almost_equal
r = np.random.randn(16)
a2, d2, d1 = pywt.wavedec(r, 'haar', level=2)

assert_array_almost_equal(a2, pywt.downcoef('a', r, wavelet='haar', level=2))
assert_array_almost_equal(d2, pywt.downcoef('d', r, wavelet='haar', level=2))
```
The second ``assert_array_almost_equal`` call above fails, but the first passes.

issue_title: Piezo and elastic data sets
issue_body:
@kylebystrom 

I made some changes to the "datasets" package of matminer. Some require your attention:

- Note that git-lfs seemed to be a big hassle and so I decided to just remove it altogether. Hopefully a normal "pull" operation will just fix everything on your end, but if not you may need to re-clone. Let's skip git-lfs from now on unless it really seems necessary, sorry for the hassle but now we know.
- Note that I added tests to the package
- Note that I added a note of the original reference both in the CSV and method
- **Needs your attention**: The elastic_tensor dataset is nice in that it contains both formula and structure columns that can be used to generate descriptors. However, the "piezo" and "dielectric_constant" data sets don't have these columns (they are embedded in "meta"). Can you pull these columns out of meta, along with anything else that looks useful to have as its own column? You'll need to update the unit tests afterward. Make sure to preserve the comment line at top that gives the reference.

issue_title: some remaining items for DosFeaturizer
issue_body:
@dyllamt @albalu 

* move to featurizers/dos.py (instead of bandstructure.py)
* the xbm_location_i should probably return fractional, not Cartesian coords. Can't think of any good reason for it to be Cartesian
* the coordination variable in ``get_cbm_vbm_scores`` is way too hacky. Only works for tet vs oct, uses a custom function, etc. Remove this var. If someone want this function they should code it well (works for multiple environments, etc), or keep it inside their own test code. Note that there is another issue open for a featurizer that takes in a site and returns back a string label for the coordination number of that site.
* get_cbm_vbm_scores() should explain in the docs what it's doing

issue_title: Python 2 support
issue_body:
Would you accept a Python 2 support PR?

It looks like not a lot of code changes are required.

I played around a little, here are my findings:

* the Cython code just compiles and seems to work on Python 2 unchanged

* the ``__all__`` in the ``__init__.py`` fails, because I think ``__all__`` in Cython is a unicode object, something Python 2 doesn't like. I'd advocate changing the ``__init__.py`` to explicitly import everything needed instead of doing a ``from .. import *``. This way you are in full control of what's exposed in the public API.

* the test suite relies on some features from Python 3:

  * in particular ``unitest.mock``, which is available as ``mock`` on PyPI so that's easy
  
  *  ``assertRaisesRegex`` which is missing. It'd need to be backported, or the test rewritten
      as the regexes used aren't complicated.
 
  *  ``subTest``, either backported or the test rewritten.

  I'm a fan of pytest myself so alternatively we can port the tests over over to that; it's compatible
  with Python 2 and Python 3. I'd be happier doing that instead.

* I recommend a tool like tox so we can easily run the tests on both Pythons.

* the Makefile is geared towards Python 3, but doesn't do all that much. I just called the build_ext command myself with Python 2. We could cruftify the makefile to support Python 2. Alternatively we could also just simply document the few standard Python commands needed; tox should care of building stuff and running stuff anyway and that's a simple command.

issue_title: Centralize Custom Exceptions
issue_body:
Pulp Smash defines a variety of custom exceptions. This is appropriate: if a standard exception does not fit a situation well, one should define a new exception and raise it instead of executing `raise Exception`. The problem is that those custom exceptions are scattered throughout the codebase. Let's do the following:

1. Define a new module, `pulp_smash.exceptions`.
2. Move all custom exceptions into this module, and adjust references to those exceptions as necessary.
3. Ensure the various make recipes reference this new module. This means at least `make docs-html` and `make lint-pylint`.

issue_title: Multiple `ConnectionField` fields do not function as expected
Add clarification around concurrency and parallelization
issue_body:
Hey guys,

I've been wondering what is the concurrency behavior / story of graphene but I don't see anything mentioned in the code base. 

A few questions on my mind:
- Has it been a design constrain yet at all? Was the API / architecture designed with this potential concurrency down the road?
- Can any schema be resolved in parallel in a thread pool? like most sql drivers do
- Can we also use gevent or twisted for I/O calls that support it? like making network calls
- Can we mix and match the 2 approaches?

Since Python does not really shine out-of-the-box when it comes to parallelization, I think having clarity would help contributors to build performant integrations with different frameworks. Also it would help users to decide to use Graphene or not.

In my case, I could potentially use the base NodeJS implementation to call over RPCs into my python services, but we are mostly a python shop and I don't want to introduce yet another language (esp Node :p ) unless I really need to.

If concurrency hasn't been given yet much thought but you think it should, especially in the v1 context, I would be very happy to brainstorm here with the broader community :)

Cheers,
J

issue_title: [Feature request] Support for query complexity analysis
issue_body:
(I plan to implement it in the coming days if there's no strong objection/concern, this issue is mainly for discussion purpose)

Sangria has implemented a query complexity analysis, which is described here: http://sangria-graphql.org/learn/#query-complexity-analysis. We would like to implement something similar with Graphene.

We were able to implement a simple version (every node counts as 1) without modifying graphene code. But in order to implement a more complex and realistic version, some works need to be done inside Graphene.

The main problem we are facing is that we cannot figure out a way to add a custom property to nodes defined in our schema, something like:

```python
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
  my_field = graphene.Field(
    MyType,
    description='some description',
    resolver=my_func,
    complexity=2,
  )
```

Even if we can overload `graphene.Field` to accept the complexity parameter in the constructor, we don't really get `Query` or `graphene.Field` back in the schema. Instead, we get `GraphQLField` or `GrapheneObjectType`, which already filtered that out.

There are a few different ways to make it possible:

1. Allow users to add custom properties to schema definitions (kind of like how we do `args` in `graphene.Field`), which will be kept in the schema. So the user could call `graphql.parse`, and then calculate the complexity with parsed AST and complexity info from the schema.

2. Add a `complexity` property to `graphene.Field`, which is default to `1` and could be either a number or a callable with parameter `args` that returns a number. Then, when we parse the query in `graphql-core`, pass in a `max_complexity` or something similar, calculate the complexity of the query, and reject the query if calculated complexity exceeds it.

Please do let me know if you have any concerns or suggestions. Thanks!

issue_title: Convert from boto to boto3
Reset login attempts on successful login
issue_body:
Hi @aleksihakli

We ran in to the problem that failed login attempts do not get deleted on successful user login.

Let's say a user misstypes his password from time to time but between the failed logins he logs in correctly. Like this the failed logins count up and it could happen that in the future when he fails to login he gets locked out on his first try.

We fixed this with a custom login view:

```python
class CustomLoginView(LoginView):
    def form_valid(self, form):
        valid = super().form_valid(form)
        user = form.get_user()

        if valid:
            reset(username=user.email)

        return valid
```

Would it make sense to have such a functionality as a option in `settings.py`?

issue_title: Allow custom weight_decay in TF-Hub Image modules
issue_body:
**Summary:** Currently there is no way to provide weight decay to Image modules of TF-Hub - it is hardcoded inside the model. This curtails the customisability of the model as _weight decay is one of the core hyperparameters_ in any model.

**Reference:** Stackoverflow discussion : [link](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/49944223/tensorflow-hub-image-modules-clarity-on-preprocessing-and-output-values)

**Details:** 
Current implementation of image modules are using TF-Slim - and the weight decay is hardcoded (for e.g. for Inception V3 it is 0.00004). Currently i'm implementing transfer learning for a geology dataset with Inception - and would really appreciate the capability to tune weight decay.

p.s: TF-Hub is an awesome idea - kudos to the TF Hub developers. Special thanks to @arnoegw for swift clarifications!

issue_title: [netdata] issues when multiple BRs are configured the same prefixes (or routes) with different stable flag
issue_body:
Multiple BRs MAY be configured with the same prefixes (or routes) of different stable flags, different priorities etc. That is, in theory, one prefix may include both stable and unstable borderroutertlv, each of which may also include multiple borderrouterentries.

Current `GetNextOnMeshPrefix()` would always get the BRs in the first `BorderRouterTlv` of the Prefix.  Another impact  is on`IsOnMeshPrefixConsistent() ` evaluation and may cause repeating SRV_DATA.ntf. Similar impact on external routes.

issue_title: MbedTls global heap constructor
issue_body:
When adding a C++ heap implementation for mbedtls (#1970) a global c++ object was added.

https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/1970/files#diff-82c87f674ffd8fa01d57e705c0b369bfR45

The initialization of this object should be done in the `MbedTls` object constructor.

issue_title: Possible issue in `Leader::ExternalRouteLookup()`
issue_body:
I am not sure if this is really an issue/bug, but thought it'd be safer to mention it so it can be checked... :)

In `Leader::ExternalRouteLookup()` method, we go through the network data tlvs in `mTlvs` and check any `PrefixTlv` to see if prefix matches our destination, and if it does we then go through sub-tlvs of the prefix tlv and check for preference, rout cost , etc.

We use the variable `cur` in an outer loop to to go through all the tlvs and then in an inner for loop we use `cur` to go through the prefix sub-tlvs.  If I am not mistaken, at the end of the inner sub-tlv loop, the `cur` will be at the end of all sub-tlvs of the `PrefixTlv` which should be start of next tlv. When/If we go back to the outer loop, we will do a `cur = cur->GetNext()` as part of the loop iteration which is intended to jump over the `PrefixTlv` but I think it will skip the next tlv. @jwhui  is there something I am missing? Thanks.

If this is actually an issue, fix should be simple: We can use a different local var like `subCur` for inner loop.

issue_title: Pmag funtion to obtain rotation angles from rotation vectors
issue_body:
So the idea is that there are dogeo and dotilt functions that take the original vector and rotation angles, turn those angles into a rotation matrix and then return the rotated vector. I'm wondering if there is a function in pmag that takes in the original vector and rotated vector and solves backwards for the rotation angles used to make the matrix? If not does someone know the appropriate equations to get azimuth and dip out of the origional vector and the rotated one? @ltauxe? I tried solving backwards myself but gave up after half an hour when I realized the pmag.dogeo function translated the rotation matrix into Cartesian and I got sick of trig. Though as far as I can tell there is a possible solution as there are 3 equations (3x3 matrix multiplied by a 3x1 vector so 3 equations) and 2 unknowns (azimuth and dip).

The application of this function is dominantly for the conversion scripts I've been working on. A number of data formats outside our own save dec, inc, geo_dec, geo_inc, tilt_dec, tilt_inc and it would be good not to loose the orientation data bundled in those different vectors when translating to MagIC for upload.

issue_title: Document defaults for CallOptions
issue_body:
During the usability study, participants intuitively understood the role of the CallOptions parameter, and its implications on their API requests.

However, for those that investigated further, it was not clear what the default CallOptions setting were, and thus it was difficult to predict the behavior of their requests without first manually setting the options.

**Recommendation:** Ensure that default CallOptions are accurately and clearly documented. If default options differ by API request, consider adding the defaults to each method’s reference documentation, so user can accurately predict the behavior of their request.

/cc @rok987 
/cc @andrewmacvean
/cc @tbetbetbe

issue_title: Improvement: Use credential_process instead of locally storing credentials
issue_body:
Hi.

Instead of storing the credentials in the .aws/credentials, we could adapt this lib to use the credential_process method of authentication (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/topic/config-vars.html#sourcing-credentials-from-external-processes).

To do that, we have to change the script to print to stdout:
```
{
  "Version": 1,
  "AccessKeyId": "ACCESS_KEY",
  "SecretAccessKey": "SECRET_KEY",
  "SessionToken": "SESSION_TOKEN",
  "Expiration": "EXPIRATION"
}
```

And then add a line to the .aws/config file:
```
[profile AWS_PROFILE_NAME]
region=AWS_REGION_NAME
credential-process=aws-google-auth -r AWS_ROLE_ARN
```

It would work even better if we stored the password of the user (#26), so we could do things like auto-renew of the credentials.
We could also cache the credentials output somewhere and use it if it is not expired yet, to avoid unnecessary requests.

issue_title: python3 doesn't have file type like python2 does
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Anonymous**

---

Using python3.3 with a cmd2.py modified by 2to3.
There are two instances were cmd2 checks whether some object is of type file. There is apparently no builtin type file.

I fixed the issue this way:

try:
    import io
    file = io.TextIOWrapper
except ImportError:
    pass # Python2

But only tried it on python3 version.

Also your documentation claims that cmd has shortcuts @ and !: there doesn't appear to be any @, but there is a ? shortcut.

---
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/catherinedevlin/cmd2/issue/4

issue_title: Remove extra help_* methods for maintainability
issue_body: Currently there are several help_* methods which don't really need to exist and are just adding some maintenance headache.  They could be replaced with docstrings for the associated do_* method.

issue_title: Enum class doesn't show in the correct namespace/submodule in help of methods that uses them
Wrong error triggered in calls with keyword arguments
Is here a str object leaked ?
issue_body:
I just started using pybind, and new to C++11 (but experienced with old school C++), to my current understanding to the semantics, the returned `str` object from `pybind11::str(format)` at this line:
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/0aef6422a349e498410c4a06b9decc04addd8c25/include/pybind11/numpy.h#L426
should be *moved* to `object args` at this line:
https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/0aef6422a349e498410c4a06b9decc04addd8c25/include/pybind11/numpy.h#L443

And if so, given `args.release()` is called here, I suspect the intermediate `str` object should keep its refcnt==1 after both function returned.

Am I wrong?
Sorry I'm not familiar enough with pybind or Py C-API to code verify this.

issue_title: Keyword arguments mismatch with C++ function arguments
issue_body:
I have a C++ function with the following signature:
```C++
spv_float well_path_ident(
	ulong nx, ulong ny, spv_float coord, spv_float zcorn,
	spv_float well_info, bool include_well_nodes, const char* strat_traits = "online_tops",
	const ulong min_split_threshold = 0, spv_ulong hit_idx = nullptr
);
```
Here `ulong` is `std::size_t`, and `spv_float, spv_ulong` are `std::shared_ptr` pointers to my custom array type which is inherited from `pybind11::array_t`. I've written custom converters for it that are very similar to `Eigen` binding code. Converters are working as expected in my tests.

Next, I export the function above with the following snippet:
```C++
m.def("well_path_ident", &well_path_ident,
	"nx"_a, "ny"_a, "coord"_a, "zcorn"_a, "well_info"_a, "include_well_nodes"_a = true,
	"mesh_traits"_a = "online_tops", "min_split_threshold"_a = 0, "hit_idx"_a = nullptr
);
```
Everything compiles just fine! `pybind11` correctly counts arguments of `well_path_ident` -- if I make a mistake (for ex. forget one argument) it will notify me with an error at compile time.

But after module is loaded in Python here's what I get with `help(my_module)` command:
```
well_path_ident(...)
        well_path_ident(*args, **kwargs)
        Overloaded function.

        1. well_path_ident(nx: int, ny: int, numpy.ndarray[float64], numpy.ndarray[float64], numpy.ndarray[float64], coord: bool, zcorn: unicode, well_info: int, numpy.ndarray[uint64]) -> numpy.ndarray[float64]
```
(I have one more overload of this function, but it has the same issue).

As you can see, keyword arguments "skip" `numpy.ndarray[float64]` (spv_float) for some reason, and starting from "coord" they bind to wrong C++ function arguments.

I feel that this is related in some way to custom type conversion code, but what really can cause such issue?
Btw, when I try to call this function from Python, I get an exception:
```
RuntimeError: Unknown internal error occurred
```
And actual C++ function isn't called, I can't get into it in debugger.

issue_title: Regression when binding alias functions
Unable to run example test using Python 2.7.12
issue_body:
### Steps for reproduce:
 1. Following steps from README.rst install `pip install pytest_reportportal`. 
 2. Than create `sample_test.py` from readme file and `pytest.ini` configuration file.
 3. Run test with command `pytest sample_test.py`.

There are no any issues with `Python3`. But with `Python2` you might get following issue:
```
 $ pytest sample_test.py 
=============================== test session starts ================================
platform linux2 -- Python 2.7.12, pytest-3.2.0, py-1.5.2, pluggy-0.4.0
rootdir: /ReportPoral, inifile: pytest.ini
plugins: reportportal-1.0.1
collected 1 item s

sample_test.py F

===================================== FAILURES =====================================
_____________________________________ test_one _____________________________________

    def test_one():
>       logger.info("Case1. Step1")

sample_test.py:18: 
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 
/usr/lib/python2.7/logging/__init__.py:1167: in info
    self._log(INFO, msg, args, **kwargs)
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 

self = <pytest_reportportal.rp_logging.RPLogger object at 0x7f31745b5c10>
level = 20, msg = 'Case1. Step1', args = (), exc_info = None, extra = None
stack_info = False, attachment = None

    def _log(self, level, msg, args,
             exc_info=None, extra=None, stack_info=False, attachment=None):
        """
            Low-level logging routine which creates a LogRecord and then calls
            all the handlers of this logger to handle the record.
            """
        sinfo = None
        if logging._srcfile:
            # IronPython doesn't track Python frames, so findCaller raises an
            # exception on some versions of IronPython. We trap it here so that
            # IronPython can use logging.
            try:
>               fn, lno, func, sinfo = self.findCaller(stack_info)
E               TypeError: findCaller() takes exactly 1 argument (2 given)

agent-python-pytest/pytest_reportportal/rp_logging.py:23: TypeError
============================= 1 failed in 0.07 seconds =============================
```

The root cause is obvious - different semantics of internal `logging` methods. 
Need to investigate changes which lead to this problem.

issue_title: Auto venv details
issue_body:
In #2885 I did not change the name of the venv created by `*-auto`. The reason for this is I believe we expressed some concern about people reaching into the venv to run `letsencrypt` directly, e.g. `~/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt`.

Do we want to rename the venv? If not, we need to revert the change made on this [line](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/certbot/cli.py#L40).

issue_title: Add test to monitor for Mozilla TLS Config Changes
issue_body:
We use [Mozilla's intermediate TLS config](https://wiki.mozilla.org/Security/Server_Side_TLS#Version_History) in Apache and Nginx. When these recommendations change, we should make sure we're notified so we can update our code. I've just been following https://github.com/mozilla/server-side-tls to keep an eye on any changes, but there's a lot of talk around TLS 1.3 which is generating a lot of noise for me. We should automate this check.

To do this, we should add a script to `tests/` that checks if anything has changed. There's lots of ways to do this, but they way I'd probably do it is by querying https://statics.tls.security.mozilla.org/server-side-tls-conf.json. We could just check if the file changes at all or if the intermediate config has changed which might result in fewer false positives at the expense of some extra parsing. I'm fine with either approach for a first version.

If the file changes, the test should fail and require us to update the test for it to continue to work again. A simple tox environment should be set up to run this test. See the code in `testenv:modification` for an example.

After the above has landed in `master`, a PR should be made against `test-everything` to add a `.travis.yml` job to test this. I'd like this test to run in `test-everything` rather than `master` so it doesn't slow down and confuse devs when they open PRs against master.

issue_title: Allow querying an account without calling new_account in ACMEv2
issue_body:
If you do this with boulder, you get:
```
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 130, in query_registration
    return self._send_recv_regr(regr, messages.UpdateRegistration())
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 75, in _send_recv_regr
    response = self._post(regr.uri, body)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 92, in _post
    return self.net.post(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 1109, in post
    return self._post_once(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 1123, in _post_once
    return self._check_response(response, content_type=content_type)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 983, in _check_response
    raise messages.Error.from_json(jobj)
acme.messages.Error: urn:ietf:params:acme:error:malformed :: The request message was malformed :: No Key ID in JWS header
```
This is because [querying an account requires kid to be set](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-13#section-6.2), but if [self.net.account isn't set, kid isn't included](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/c129ab2965f7ec4edf173b1cd900bd5add13cd2a/acme/acme/client.py#L938).

An easy fix for this would be to set `self.net.account = regr` in the beginning of `query_registration`, at least for ACMEv2. Doing it at the end of `query_registration` to store the account as given by the ACME server isn't a bad idea either.

This is useful for the scenario in #6246.

issue_title: HTTPServiceUnavailable on endpoint not available
issue_body:
When endpoint-management is reaching out to get service config i'm getting a 403, I'm still unsure what i've changed to get this but it doesn't really matter for now. [This raises a ServiceConfigException](https://github.com/cloudendpoints/endpoints-management-python/blob/f9d4cda808bbd79bd4d7bcb63d624b102e12ec98/endpoints_management/config/service_config.py#L81-L85).

This gets caught at the wsgi init layer, but instead of propogating the error it [returns a webob.exc.HTTPServiceUnavailable](https://github.com/cloudendpoints/endpoints-management-python/blob/f9d4cda808bbd79bd4d7bcb63d624b102e12ec98/endpoints_management/control/wsgi.py#L123-L130).

What this means is that in my entrypoint file like: 

```
api = endpoints.api_server([MyAPI])
```

the `api` object is now a `HTTPServiceUnavailable` object and endpoints tries to route traffic to it ending up with errors that look like: 
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/base/alloc/tmpfs/dynamic_runtimes/python27g/7894e0c59273b2b7/python27/python27_lib/versions/1/google/appengine/runtime/wsgi.py", line 267, in Handle
    result = handler(dict(self._environ), self._StartResponse)
  File "/base/data/home/apps/f~teg-jamie-testing/listings:20180625t231203.410697777093027702/vendor/webob/exc.py", line 271, in __init__
    self.headers.extend(headers)
  File "/base/data/home/apps/f~teg-jamie-testing/listings:20180625t231203.410697777093027702/vendor/webob/multidict.py", line 233, in extend
    for k, v in other:
TypeError: 'instancemethod' object is not iterable
```
because the 2nd argument to `HTTPServiceUnavailable.__init__` is headers, not `start_response` as expected.

I'm not entirely sure what good behaviour here is, it makes sense that this needs to return an error but i really want it to happen before i start serving requests (actually i want to fail the deployment based on it).

My current solution i think is going to be like (pseudo): 
```
_api = endpoints.api_server([MyAPI])
if isinstance(api, webob.exc.Exception): 
    def fail(environ, start_response):
        logging.warning("Failed to start API", exc_info=_api.something())
        # return 503 with some actual information
        
    api = fail
else:
    api = _api 
```
so that at least I get a traceback that I can debug failures with because the current error tells me nothing.

Thanks

issue_title: Use constant lock expiration for all hash-time-locks of the same transfer
issue_body:
```
In the first interation of Raiden unlock had to be called on a per
channel basis, and the operation was only allowed for a channel on the
closed state. Because the unlock was on a per channel basis, every
mediator had to use a lower expiration for the lock it sent in respect
to the lock it received.

The hash-time-lock expiration was always decrease by a configuration
variable named reveal timeout, the setting was in number of blocks, and
represented how many blocks were required by the node to learn about an
on-chain unlock on the payee channel, and call unlock on the payer
channel. The reveal timeout setting included how many blocks the node
requires to learn about a new confirmed block, for the payee unlock,
plus how many blocks were required to successfully mine two
transactions, the close and the unlock on the payer channel.

The above dynamics changed with the introduction of the secret registry.
With the secret registry the channel no longer needs to be closed in
order to call unlock, once the secret is registered this information can
be used by the channel smart contract to unlock the any hash-time-lock
on-chain. Additionally the secret registry allowed the lock for *all*
mediators to be unlocked at the same time, this difference turns out to
be crucial.

With the secret registry, the semantics of the reveal timeout changed
from the time to learn about a new block and to mine two trasanctions,
to just the time required to mine the secret registration transaction.

The secret registration happens if a node learns about the secret
off-chain while the hash-time-lock is in the danger zone, where the
danger zone is a block close enough to the lock's expiration in which it
may take too long for the secret to be registered in time, becaase of
the risk of losing the payment, the node must send a transaction to
register the secret on-chain.

The above means a node will send a secret register transaction
regardless of how many blocks are left for the expiration of lock, be it
unhappy case of a single block or the happy case of `reveal_timeouts`
blocks. The rationale is that a node wants to receive the payment, and
if the locks is nearing expiration it should try to register the secret
to secure the payment. (Note that the unhappy case can be improved, but
the race condition cannot be fixed)

As a consequence, if the lock expiration decreases through the route, as
it was done with the first iteration, the secret registration may happen
too late for hop which sent the transaction, but soon enough for
previous hops. This means that for a path like A-B-C, the node C can
learn about a secret at block `n` which is `5` apart from the lock's
expiration, and the transaction can be mined at block `n+6`, C will not
receive the payment but if the lock expiration for A-B was larger B can
receive the payment.

The fix is to make lock's expiration constant. The initiator is
responsible to choose a value that is sufficiently large to allow a
transfer to be routed through the network without expiring, and low
enough so that it's not reject by too many channels with a low
settlement timeout. Once the target receive the hash-time-lock it must
include the lock's expiration in the secret request, which in turn is
verified by the transfer initiator, where it's of interest of the
initiator to make sure the expiration was constant through out the path,
otherwise the target would be at risk of not receiving the transfer.
```

issue_title: DDD Energy interpolation idea.
issue_body:
Im not sure if you need this but i thought that it might be of interest so here goes:

Since there was no energy interpolation function implemented in the DDD i took it upon myself to create a function that as of now is working for the purposes i need it to.
I have copied the original DDD.load_ddd(directory) and changed it to generate simply a list of energies and a dictionary of the ddd data but including FWHM 1 & 2 and the factor, as such:

```
def load_ddd(self, directory):
        x_data = []
        y_data = []
        z_data = []
        a_data = []
        b_data = []
        c_data = []
        points = [[], []]
        ddd = {}
        max_dist = []
        items = glob.glob(directory)
        for item in items:
            x_data = []
            y_data = []
            z_data = []
            a_data = []
            b_data = []
            with open(item, 'r') as f:
                data = f.read()
            lines = data.split('\n')
            for n, line in enumerate(data.split("\n")):
                if line.find("energy") is not -1:
                    energy = float(line.split()[1])
                    c_data.append(energy)
                if line.find('!') is -1 and line.find('#') is -1:
                    break
            for i in range(n, len(lines)):
                if len(lines[i]) < 3:
                    continue
                point = [float(s) for s in lines[i].split()]
                x_data.append(point[0])
                y_data.append(point[1])
                z_data.append(point[2])
                a_data.append(point[3])
                b_data.append(point[4])

            
            ddd[energy] = np.array([x_data, y_data, z_data, a_data, b_data]).T
            
        self.ddd = ddd
        self.energy = np.asarray(c_data)
```

with that i then created a function called DDD.Generate_interpolated_DDD(energy), that creates a part of the same self.ddd directory but with a energy that the user defines, with simple **linear** interpolation, as such:

```
def Generate_interpolated_DDD(self, energy):
        nearest_energy = self.energy[np.abs(self.energy-energy).argmin()]
        
        if nearest_energy-energy >= 0:
            try:
                nearest_energy = self.energy[np.abs(self.energy-energy).argmin()-1]
            except:
                print('Energy range of .ddd files inadequate')      
        try:
            nearest_energy_2 = self.energy[np.abs(self.energy-energy).argmin()+1]
        except:
            print('Energy range of .ddd files inadequate')
        
        ddd_1 = self.ddd[nearest_energy]
        ddd_2 = self.ddd[nearest_energy_2]

        range_1 = ddd_1[np.where(ddd_1 == np.max(ddd_1[:,2]))[0], 0]
        range_2 = ddd_2[np.where(ddd_2 == np.max(ddd_2[:,2]))[0], 0]
        scaling_factor = range_2/range_1
        lista=[]
        
        depths = ddd_1[:,0]
        
        dist = ddd_2[:,0]*(scaling_factor*133/140)
        dist = dist[dist < ddd_2[-1,0]]
        for dist in dist:
            nearest_dist_nr = np.abs(ddd_1[:, 0]*scaling_factor-dist).argmin()
            nearest_dist_2_nr = np.abs(ddd_2[:, 0]-dist).argmin()
            
            if ddd_1[nearest_dist_nr, 0]*scaling_factor-dist <= 0:
                nearest_dist_nr -= 1
            
            if ddd_2[nearest_dist_2_nr, 0]-dist <= 0:
                nearest_dist_2_nr -= 1
                
            if nearest_dist_nr == len(ddd_1[:,0])-1:
                nearest_dist_nr -= 1
                
            if nearest_dist_2_nr == len(ddd_2[:,0])-1:
                nearest_dist_2_nr -= 1
                
            temp_ddd_1 = []
            temp_ddd_2 = []
            for number in range(1, 5):
                temp_ddd_1.append(azz.interpolate(dist/scaling_factor ,ddd_1[nearest_dist_nr, 0] ,ddd_1[nearest_dist_nr+1, 0], ddd_1[nearest_dist_nr, number], ddd_1[nearest_dist_nr+1, number]))
                temp_ddd_2.append(azz.interpolate(dist ,ddd_2[nearest_dist_2_nr, 0] ,ddd_2[nearest_dist_2_nr+1, 0], ddd_2[nearest_dist_2_nr, number], ddd_2[nearest_dist_2_nr+1, number]))
            
            lista.append([float(dist/(scaling_factor*133/140)), azz.interpolate(energy, 130, 140, temp_ddd_1[0], temp_ddd_2[0]), azz.interpolate(energy, 130, 140, temp_ddd_1[1], temp_ddd_2[1]), azz.interpolate(energy, 130, 140, temp_ddd_1[2], temp_ddd_2[2]), azz.interpolate(energy, 130, 140, temp_ddd_1[3], temp_ddd_2[3])])

        lista = np.array(lista)
        lista[np.where(lista[:,1] < 0), 1] = 0
        lista[np.where(lista[:,2] < 0), 2] = 0
        lista[np.where(lista[:,3] < 0), 3] = 0
        lista[np.where(lista[:,4] < 0), 4] = 0
        
        self.ddd[energy] = lista
```

the last part simply forces all negative values to 0.
So when running, whatever.Generate_interpolated_DDD(133), you can then call the DDD file using whatever.ddd[133] to get a interpolated DDD file for 133 MeV. I have plotted them and they look fine to me:
Cyan is 130 MeV
Magenta is 133 MeV
Yellow is 140 MeV.

issue_title: Refactor: Looking for implementation strategies to improve run time efficiency of all algorithms regardless of data type (i.e. discrete/continuous, missing data)
docs for iterator: repeat / init_epoch
issue_body:
I think people tend to get confused that when a iterator is created with repeat set to true, it's up to them to break out of the for-loop when they are ready (the iterator never stops). It seems like when people set repeat to true, they might think "yes I want to train for multiple epochs without getting the StopIterator warning".

It seems like the two modes of iteration that this library encourages are:

1. have repeat set to false, control epochs in an outer loop, call iter.init_epoch() at the start of every epoch
2. have repeat set to true, use a counter and an if statement to conditionally break out of the loop when ready

Is this roughly correct?

Happy to submit a PR to clarify this in the docs of iterator.py

### System information

- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: Binary wheel (pip, `ray-0.3.0-cp36-cp36m-macosx_10_6_intel.whl`)

issue_title: Ray Wait is waiting for all tasks to complete
issue_body:
## RL Experiments

I'm doing RL sampling right now, and I'm timing the tasks at a finer grain. I'm also using a ray version off commit 6cd02d71f8e
```
    while num_samples < max_samples:
        for i in range(num_workers - len(remaining)): 
            remaining.append(ray_rollout.remote(param_id, max_path_length))
        done, remaining = ray.wait(remaining)
        result, timestamps = ray.get(done[0])
        num_samples += len(result['rewards'])
```

Here is the rollout code: 
```
@ray.remote
def ray_rollout(policy_params, max_path_length):
    """returns rollout dictionary, id, (start, end)"""
    start_time = str(datetime.now())
    ...
    return rollout(policy_params), (start_time, str(datetime.now()))
```
However, instead of getting fine-grain tasks, the time plots look like this:

issue_title: <label> should not be used for multiple checkbox groups
issue_body:
* Package version: 1.6.1 * Django version: 1.11 * Python version: 3.5.2 * Template pack: bootstrap3 ### Description: Validation of my form HTML5 at [validator.nu](https://html5.validator.nu/) detected the following issue: > VALIDATION TROUBLE > 222.58-222.139: error: The “for” attribute of the “label” element must refer to a non-hidden form control. This is in a Bootstrap 3 multiple inline checkbox created using `InlineCheckboxes()`. ```python self.helper.layout = Layout( InlineCheckboxes('target_marketplaces'), ... ) ``` Looking at the HTML output from crispy (some code removed) ```HTML
...

Which eBay Marketplaces do you wish to target?

```` I can see that the 'for' target for the first label ("id_target_marketplaces") indeed does not exist. Comments on Stackoverflow: - [Single label for two inputs](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/14854062/single-label-for-two-inputs) and - [Two input fields inside one label](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/9004307/two-input-fields-inside-one-label) indicate that a label should be associated with one, *and only one* form input, and suggest using a `
` to group the checkbox elements and a `` for the label. ### Preferably also include: - [x] Example Django Crispy Forms code - [ ] Screenshots - [x] Actual HTML generated - [ ] Expected HTML

issue_title: dimension ordering in climt
issue_body:
I don't remember where we were discussing this, but we had decided to keep the dimension ordering to suit the dynamical core (i.,e horizontal dimensions are the fastest varying indices).

On second thought, I don't think this is the correct decision. In an MPI scenario, this would mean that 
there is communication needed to run components which require a whole column. Since the dycore will
anyway require communications (since it is not scalable to distribute arrays based on vertical levels),
this would put a huge overhead.

Even in our current scenario, this would mean array reshaping for every component other the GFS, 
which is again inefficient.

I propose we move back to the original ordering. I can do this. I presume all this would entail is changing
the ordering in the properties those components which are lat/lon aware?

issue_title: Insufficient `datacube dataset update` documentation
issue_body:
This is command line help for `datacube dataset update`

```
Usage: datacube dataset update [OPTIONS] [DATASETS]...

  Update datasets in the Data Cube

Options:
  --allow-any TEXT        Allow any changes to the specified key (a.b.c)
  -r, --match-rules PATH  Rules to be used to associate datasets with products
  -t, --dtype TEXT        Product to be associated with the datasets
  -a, --auto-match        Automatically associate datasets with products by
                          matching metadata
  --dry-run               Check if everything is ok
  -h, --help              Show this message and exit.
```

Notice how it has all the **auto-matching** rules that `datacube dataset add` has. Why are they needed? I assume that I can only update dataset that already exists, if it already exists it is already associated with some product, surely changing product of an already indexed dataset shouldn't be allowed? Or is `update` also implies possibly `add` if missing?

This brings us to the other important question:

- Exactly what aspects of dataset are safe to change?

Neither command line help, nor `readthedocs` give any indication as to what aspects of Dataset are meant to be immutable and which can be safely changed. One obvious thing that can be safely changed is dataset location on disk. Probably various bits of metadata that are meant for human consumption can also be changed (correcting a typo in a description field, that sort of thing).

In general dataset management beyond addition is fairly opaque as far as documentation goes.

issue_title: User defined timesteps
issue_body:
Allow the timestep to be user defined rather than being fixed at one year. 

Will require some thinking about how we do this e.g. do we let the user supply yearly emissions and then have FAIR linearly interpolate if they want to use smaller (or bigger) timesteps or do we make it that the emissions inputs define the timesteps with some flag to indicate whether the timeseries is days, months, years etc.

issue_title: ContextTracer.finish() does not end remaining spans
issue_body:
From reading the documentation of Tracer.finish() it looks like calling finish() on a tracer object should end all remaining spans and send them to exporters. However, it looks like the current implementation 
just [resets the internal span list](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/tracers/context_tracer.py#L54). This usually isn't a problem unless you get into a state where all spans are not ended properly.

I am proposing that we update ContextTracer.finish() to end all spans in ContextTracer._spans_list before resetting state. 

Happy to submit a PR for this change if everyone agrees on the proposal.

and noticed a difference in the response content with curl using [this](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/merge_requests.html#get-single-mr) API call. The documented API call is

Running in debug I see that python-gitlab is making this call

Is there a different syntax to request the object directly?
